title: Virtualize a 50k-row trading order book table that freezes the main thread on every tick
business_context: A crypto exchange's web order book renders up to 50,000 bid/ask rows in a React table. During volatile sessions the WebSocket pushes 30 updates/sec; each update re-renders the entire table, blocking the main thread for 400-900ms. Traders miss fills and churn. p95 input latency hit 1.2s during the last BTC flash crash.
constraints: WebSocket delivers up to 30 depth snapshots/sec, each touching up to 2,000 price levels
Must keep keyboard navigation (arrow keys) and screen-reader row semantics intact
Cannot drop frames below 50fps during scroll on a 2019 MacBook Air baseline
Backward-compatible with existing column config consumed by 4 other internal apps
No SSR for this view but it shares the design-system Table primitive used in SSR pages

before_state.description: The table maps over the full row array and re-renders on every WebSocket message; React reconciles 50k DOM nodes per tick.

issues: Renders all 50k rows into the DOM regardless of viewport height
setRows replaces the whole array so React reconciles every node each tick
No memoization: a single price change repaints the entire table
Parses and forwards full snapshots with no diffing or throttling
Each message triggers an immediate React state update with no batching window

reasoning: Naive fix: wrap each <tr> in React.memo. Rejected — memo still requires React to walk 50k elements to decide nothing changed, and creating 50k fiber nodes alone costs >200ms. Alternative: CSS content-visibility:auto to skip offscreen paint. Rejected — it skips paint but not reconciliation, so the JS freeze remains. Chosen: windowed virtualization (react-window) rendering only ~40 visible rows, fed by a ref-held ring buffer that the WS writes into, with a 60ms rAF-throttled flush. Cross-layer trace: UI renders a fixed-height List of visible indices -> a depth store applies WS deltas into a typed-array keyed by price bucket (data) -> a requestAnimationFrame coalescer flushes at most one React update per frame (async) -> column config still read from the shared Table context (config) -> tests assert only visible rows mount and that 30 messages produce <=16 renders -> rollout behind a per-symbol flag.

after_state.description: Only visible rows mount via virtualization; WS deltas mutate an off-React store and a rAF coalescer flushes a single render per frame.

files.purpose: Holds depth data outside React and coalesces WS bursts into one render per animation frame via useSyncExternalStore.
Renders only the ~40 visible rows with ARIA row/cell roles, subscribing to the coalesced store version.
Defensively caps WS forwarding to 30/s so a misbehaving feed cannot overwhelm the coalescer.

migration_strategy: Ship depthStore + virtualized OrderBook behind a `orderbook_virtualized` flag defaulting off
Enable for BTC-USD only (highest volume canary) and watch p95 input latency via RUM
Ramp to top-10 symbols, comparing frame-drop counts against the legacy table in A/B
Roll to 100% of symbols once frame drops stay <2% over 48h
Delete the legacy full-render table path and the flag after two clean weeks
testing_strategy: Fixture: replay a captured 60s BTC depth stream (1,800 messages) into applyDelta. Assert output: at most 16 React renders/sec via a render counter; only indices within [scrollTop, scrollTop+height] mount (query rendered role=row count <= 30). Side-effect assert: store.version increments exactly once per rAF, not per message. Chaos test: inject a burst of 300 messages in 100ms and assert no render exceeds 8ms (long-task observer) and the WS throttle drops the excess.
what_breaks_naive_approach: React.memo on rows still forces React to instantiate and diff 50,000 fibers each tick; the reconciliation walk alone blocks the main thread for 200ms+ before any DOM write, so input latency stays broken even though paint is skipped.

title: Fix a Next.js product page where a non-memoized context provider re-renders the entire catalog grid on hover
business_context: An e-commerce catalog page (Next.js App Router) shows a 120-tile product grid. A CartContext provider passes a new object value on every render, so hovering any tile (which updates a hovered-id in the same provider) re-renders all 120 tiles plus their price-formatting. Lighthouse TBT is 680ms and merchants report sluggish hover on the PLP, dropping add-to-cart rate 4%.
constraints: Grid must stay a Client Component for hover/quick-add interactivity
Cart state and hover state currently share one provider consumed in 30+ places
Cannot change the public CartContext hook signature used across the app
Price formatting uses Intl.NumberFormat which is expensive at 120x per render
Must keep Server Component data fetching for the initial grid HTML

before_state.description: One context provider holds both cart items and transient hover state; its value object is recreated each render, invalidating all consumers.

issues: value object is a new reference every render so all consumers re-render
Transient hoveredId lives in the same provider as durable cart items
Hovering one tile re-renders all 120 grid tiles
Builds a new Intl.NumberFormat per render (120 allocations per hover)
Subscribes to the whole cart context just to read hoveredId
Re-renders even when neither its product nor its hovered state changed

reasoning: Naive fix: wrap the provider value in useMemo. Rejected — it stops object-identity churn but hoveredId still lives in the shared context, so every hover still re-renders all 120 consumers that read the context. Alternative: lift hover into a parent useState and pass isHovered as a prop to each tile. Rejected — the parent then re-renders the whole grid on each hover anyway. Chosen: split contexts — a stable CartContext (durable, memoized) and a separate HoverContext that exposes a subscribe API so tiles read their own hover state without re-rendering siblings; memoize the Intl formatter at module scope. Cross-layer trace: Server Component fetches products and streams HTML (data) -> Client grid mounts tiles wrapped in React.memo (UI) -> each tile subscribes to HoverContext for only its id via a selector (state) -> module-level formatter avoids per-render allocation (compute) -> useCart signature preserved by re-exporting from the split provider (config) -> tests assert hovering tile A renders exactly 2 tiles (A and previous) -> rollout behind a flag.

after_state.description: Cart and hover are separate contexts; hover uses a selector subscription so only the entering and leaving tiles re-render; formatter is module-scoped.

files.purpose: Holds only durable cart state with a memoized value so cart consumers do not re-render on hover.
Provides per-id hover subscription so only the tile whose boolean flips re-renders, not all siblings.
Memoized tile reading only its own hover boolean and reusing a module-level Intl formatter.

migration_strategy: Introduce HoverProvider alongside CartProvider behind a `split_hover_context` flag
Re-export useCart from the slimmed provider so no call sites change
Enable on a 5% PLP traffic canary, watching TBT and hover INP in RUM
Ramp to 100% once INP p75 drops below 200ms
Remove hoveredId from the old CartContext shape after one release
testing_strategy: Fixture: render a 120-product grid with a render-count probe on each tile. Assert output: hovering tile #40 then #41 triggers exactly 2 tile re-renders (40 leaves, 41 enters); cart consumer count stays 0. Side-effect assert: only one Intl.NumberFormat instance exists (spy on constructor). Chaos test: rapid-fire 200 mouseenter events across tiles in 500ms and assert total tile renders <= 401 (no fan-out) and no dropped frame >50ms.
what_breaks_naive_approach: useMemo on the combined value fixes identity but every hover still calls setHoveredId inside the shared context, so React re-renders all 120 context consumers; the per-hover cost and 120 Intl allocations remain, so INP stays above 300ms.

title: Code-split a 2.1MB analytics dashboard bundle so the first chart paints before the export engine loads
business_context: A B2B SaaS analytics dashboard ships a single 2.1MB JS bundle that bundles Chart.js, an xlsx export engine, a PDF renderer, and a date-picker into the initial route. TTI on a mid-tier laptop is 6.4s; 38% of users never click export yet still pay its 700KB parse cost. Trial-to-paid conversion correlates with first-load speed.
constraints: Export (xlsx/PDF) must still work when invoked, just lazily
No layout shift when a chart hydrates after the shell
Webpack 5 build with an existing vendor chunk shared by 6 routes
Must keep SSR shell for SEO of public report-share links
Bundle budget: initial route JS under 350KB gzipped

before_state.description: Everything is statically imported at the top of the dashboard route, so all heavy libraries land in the initial chunk.

issues: Static imports of xlsx and pdf pull 700KB into the initial chunk
Export code parses on every load even though 38% never export
DateRangePicker library loads before first paint
Top-level import of the full xlsx library (~500KB)
Pulled into initial bundle via static import in the route

reasoning: Naive fix: move export buttons to a separate route. Rejected — export is a modal action on the same view; routing away breaks the UX and SEO share links. Alternative: mark xlsx/pdf as Webpack externals loaded from a CDN script tag. Rejected — adds a blocking network round-trip and a global, and breaks offline. Chosen: React.lazy + dynamic import() for the export handlers and date-picker, with a Suspense boundary that reserves layout space, plus a webpack magic comment to prefetch export on idle. Cross-layer trace: route renders shell + chart eagerly (UI) -> export buttons trigger import('./xlsx') on click (async) -> webpack splits xlsx/pdf into named async chunks (config) -> a requestIdleCallback prefetches them after first paint (perf) -> SSR shell unchanged for share links (data) -> tests assert initial chunk excludes xlsx -> rollout via bundle-budget CI gate.

after_state.description: Heavy libraries load on demand via dynamic import; the shell and first chart paint immediately; export prefetches on idle.

files.purpose: Loads chart eagerly but defers export and date-picker via dynamic import with a layout-reserving Suspense fallback.
Defers the 500KB xlsx library to the moment export is actually clicked.
Routes xlsx/jspdf into async-only chunks and fails CI if the initial entrypoint exceeds 350KB.
Warms the export chunk after first paint so the first export click feels instant without blocking TTI.

migration_strategy: Convert export handlers to dynamic import behind a `split_export_bundle` flag
Add the webpack splitChunks export cacheGroup and turn the perf budget to 'warning' first
Canary on 10% of dashboard sessions; compare TTI and export success rate
Flip perf budget to 'error' in CI once the initial chunk is verified <350KB
Ramp to 100% and remove static export imports and the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture: build the bundle in CI and assert via stats.json that the initial entrypoint does not include xlsx/jspdf and is <350KB gzipped. Runtime fixture: render Dashboard, assert chart is in the DOM on first paint and the export chunk is NOT yet requested (mock import()). Side-effect assert: clicking XLSX triggers exactly one import('xlsx') and one writeFile call. Chaos test: simulate import() rejection (offline) and assert the UI shows a retry toast and does not crash the dashboard.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Splitting export onto a different route breaks the in-page modal UX and the SSR share-link SEO, and a CDN-externals approach adds a render-blocking script plus an offline failure; neither reduces the initial parse cost the way an on-demand dynamic import does.

title: Eliminate RSC-to-client hydration waterfall that blocks interactivity on a news homepage for 4 seconds
business_context: A news site on Next.js App Router renders a homepage of 14 article cards. Each card is a Client Component that fetches its own personalization (read-state, bookmark) on mount, creating 14 sequential client fetches after hydration. INP is 410ms and the page is non-interactive for ~4s on 4G because hydration is gated behind these fetches. Bounce rate above the fold is 22%.
constraints: Personalization is per-user and cannot be statically cached at the CDN
Must keep article card markup server-rendered for SEO and LCP
Auth is cookie-based; personalization API requires the session
14 cards today but editors can configure up to 40
No client-side data library beyond fetch is currently approved

before_state.description: Each client card independently fetches its personalization on mount, producing N parallel-but-uncoordinated requests and blocking hydration.

issues: Server fetches article content but not personalization
Defers all per-user state to the client, one request per card
14-40 independent fetches after hydration
Each card is a client component purely to fetch personalization
Hydration cost scales linearly with card count

reasoning: Naive fix: batch the 14 fetches into one /api/personalization?ids=... call from a parent useEffect. Rejected — it still runs after hydration, still needs the cards to be client components, and still shows a flash of unpersonalized stars. Alternative: make personalization a Server Component fetch and render fully on the server. Rejected — it couples per-user data into the cacheable shell, breaking CDN caching of the article HTML. Chosen: keep cards as Server Components for content, fetch all personalization once in a server action co-located with the page using the request cookie, and pass a Map down as props; cards become pure presentational (no client JS). Cross-layer trace: page Server Component fetches articles AND batched personalization in parallel with Promise.all (data) -> personalization read with the session cookie server-side (auth) -> cards render fully on the server with no client mount fetch (UI) -> zero per-card hydration (perf) -> a small client island handles only the bookmark toggle interaction (state) -> tests assert one personalization call total -> rollout behind a flag.

after_state.description: Personalization is fetched once on the server in parallel with content; cards are server-rendered presentational; only the bookmark toggle is a tiny client island.

files.purpose: Fetches articles and a single batched personalization map server-side using the session cookie, eliminating client fetch fan-out.
Single authenticated batch call returning a Map for O(1) per-card lookup; uncached so it stays per-user.
Pure Server Component card; ships no client JS except the isolated bookmark island.
Tiny client island that hydrates only the interactive bookmark button, keeping hydration cost flat regardless of card count.

migration_strategy: Add getPersonalizationBatch and pass meta as props behind a `server_personalization` flag
Keep the old client-fetch card path available via the flag for instant rollback
Canary at 10%; compare INP and time-to-interactive in RUM against control
Ramp to 100% once INP p75 < 200ms and personalization-correctness alarms are clean
Delete the per-card useEffect fetch and the /api/personalization/:id single endpoint
testing_strategy: Fixture: render Home with 40 articles and a mocked batch API. Assert output: server HTML already contains correct read/bookmark markers (no flash). Side-effect assert: exactly one POST to /batch with all 40 ids; zero calls to the per-id endpoint. Negative test: when the session cookie is missing, assert getPersonalizationBatch returns an empty Map and cards render unpersonalized without throwing. Chaos test: batch API returns 500 and assert the page still renders article content (graceful degradation) and logs the error.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Client-side batching still runs after hydration so interactivity is still gated and cards still must be client components; making personalization a plain Server Component fetch poisons the CDN-cacheable article HTML with per-user data, breaking shared caching.

title: Stop a Vue 3 logistics map from re-rendering 8,000 vehicle markers on every GPS tick
business_context: A fleet logistics dashboard (Vue 3 + Nuxt) plots up to 8,000 vehicle markers on a Leaflet map. A reactive array of positions updates every 2s from a Server-Sent Events stream; Vue's reactivity deep-tracks the array and Leaflet re-creates all 8,000 markers per tick, spiking CPU to 100% and dropping the map to 3fps. Dispatchers cannot pan during updates.
constraints: Up to 8,000 vehicles, position updates every 2s via SSE
Only ~150 markers are in the viewport at typical zoom
Must keep marker click -> vehicle detail panel working
Nuxt SSR for the page shell; map is client-only
Cannot swap Leaflet (licensing + custom plugins depend on it)

before_state.description: Positions live in a deeply reactive ref; a watcher clears and re-adds every Leaflet marker on each SSE tick.

files.content:
<script setup lang="ts">
import { ref, watch, onMounted } from 'vue';
import L from 'leaflet';
const positions = ref<Vehicle[]>([]);
let map: L.Map; let layer: L.LayerGroup;
onMounted(() => {
  map = L.map('map').setView([40, -100], 5);
  layer = L.layerGroup().addTo(map);
  const es = new EventSource('/api/fleet/stream');
  es.onmessage = (e) => { positions.value = JSON.parse(e.data); };
});
watch(positions, (list) => {
  layer.clearLayers();
  list.forEach((v) => L.marker([v.lat, v.lng]).addTo(layer).on('click', () => openPanel(v.id)));
}, { deep: true });
</script>
<template><div id="map" /></template>
issues: deep:true watcher tracks all 8,000 objects every tick
clearLayers + re-add destroys and rebuilds every marker
Renders markers outside the viewport that no one can see

reasoning: Naive fix: drop deep:true and replace the whole array reference. Rejected — Vue still triggers the watcher and we still clear+rebuild all 8,000 Leaflet markers, which is the real cost, not the reactivity tracking. Alternative: throttle the watcher to every 6s. Rejected — dispatchers need 2s freshness; throttling hides stale positions. Chosen: store positions in a shallowRef (no deep tracking), keep a stable Map<id, L.Marker>, and on each tick only setLatLng on existing markers / add-remove the delta, and only render markers within the current map bounds using Leaflet's moveend bounds. Cross-layer trace: SSE delivers full snapshot (data) -> a diff computes added/moved/removed ids (compute) -> existing markers get setLatLng, new ones created, gone ones removed (UI) -> shallowRef avoids deep reactivity overhead (state) -> a bounds filter limits live markers to viewport+buffer (perf) -> click handler bound once per marker, not rebuilt (interaction) -> tests assert marker objects are reused across ticks.

after_state.description: shallowRef positions, a persistent marker registry updated by diff with setLatLng, and viewport-bounded rendering keep the map at 60fps.

files.content:
<script setup lang="ts">
import { shallowRef, onMounted, onUnmounted } from 'vue';
import L from 'leaflet';
import { reconcileMarkers } from '../lib/markerReconciler';
const positions = shallowRef<Vehicle[]>([]);
let map: L.Map; let layer: L.LayerGroup;
let es: EventSource;
const registry = new Map<string, L.Marker>();
function render() {
  reconcileMarkers(layer, registry, positions.value, map.getBounds(), (id) => openPanel(id));
}
onMounted(() => {
  map = L.map('map').setView([40, -100], 5);
  layer = L.layerGroup().addTo(map);
  map.on('moveend', render);
  es = new EventSource('/api/fleet/stream');
  es.onmessage = (e) => { positions.value = JSON.parse(e.data); render(); };
});
onUnmounted(() => { es?.close(); });
</script>
<template><div id="map" /></template>
files.purpose: Uses shallowRef and delegates to a reconciler that reuses markers and respects map bounds, re-rendering on data and pan.
Diffs the snapshot against existing markers, moving in-view ones with setLatLng and pruning out-of-view/gone ones.

migration_strategy: Land the reconciler + shallowRef behind a `fleet_marker_diff` flag for the map page
Enable for one low-traffic region first; verify positions stay accurate vs legacy
Compare CPU and fps via a perf overlay on canary vs control
Ramp to all regions once fps stays >=55 during ticks
Remove the deep watcher path and the flag after a clean week
testing_strategy: Fixture: feed 3 consecutive SSE snapshots of 8,000 vehicles where 100 move, 5 appear, 5 disappear. Assert output: after tick 2 the same L.Marker instance is reused for unchanged ids (identity check via the registry). Side-effect assert: layer.addLayer called only 5 times (new) and removeLayer 5 times (gone), not 8,000. Negative test: a vehicle outside bounds is never added to the layer. Chaos test: SSE emits a malformed payload; assert render is skipped and the prior markers remain on the map.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Swapping to a non-deep ref still fires the watcher and the clearLayers+rebuild of 8,000 markers, which is the dominant cost; throttling to 6s sacrifices the 2s freshness dispatchers depend on. Only marker reuse + viewport bounding removes the per-tick O(8000) work.

title: Memoize a derived selector so a fintech transactions table stops recomputing a 30k-row aggregate on every keystroke
business_context: A fintech app shows a 30,000-row transactions table with a live search box. Each keystroke recomputes category totals, running-balance, and a sorted filtered list inside the render body, so typing one character costs 220ms and the input lags visibly. Compliance analysts type fast; the lag causes mistyped account filters and wasted review time.
constraints: Up to 30k transactions held in memory client-side
Search must remain debounce-free for compliance auditability of keystrokes
Running-balance must be recomputed correctly when filters change
React 18, Redux Toolkit store already in place
Sort + filter + aggregate must stay consistent (no stale totals)

before_state.description: Filtering, sorting, and aggregation run inline in the component body on every render, including every keystroke.

issues: filter+sort+map+reduce over 30k rows run on every keystroke
spread in reduce creates 30k intermediate objects per render
No memoization; identical inputs recompute fully

reasoning: Naive fix: wrap each computation in useMemo keyed on [all, q]. Rejected — it helps within a render but every keystroke changes q, so all four passes still rerun fully; the real win must come from not reprocessing unchanged data. Alternative: move filtering to a web worker. Rejected — 30k string-includes is fast enough on the main thread once we stop redundant work and avoid object spreads; a worker adds serialization latency on every keystroke. Chosen: a memoized reselect-style selector that computes the sorted base list once (independent of q), then a second memo that only re-filters the pre-sorted list and computes totals with mutation-free but allocation-light reducers; running balance computed in the same single pass. Cross-layer trace: store holds raw list (data) -> selectSortedTxns memoized on list identity sorts once (compute) -> selectView memoized on [sortedList, q] filters + aggregates + balances in one pass (compute) -> component reads memoized view (UI) -> input stays responsive (perf) -> tests assert sort runs once across 5 keystrokes.

after_state.description: Memoized selectors split stable sort from per-query filter; a single pass computes filtered rows, running balance, and totals without object spreads.

files.purpose: Splits the expensive stable sort (cached on list identity) from a single-pass per-query filter/balance/aggregate.
Consumes the memoized per-instance view selector so unchanged inputs skip recomputation.

migration_strategy: Add selectors behind a `memoized_txn_view` flag, keeping inline path as fallback
Enable for 10% of analyst sessions; measure input latency via PerformanceObserver
Verify totals/balances match the legacy inline computation byte-for-byte in shadow
Ramp to 100% once keystroke latency p95 < 30ms
Remove inline computation and the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture: 30k seeded transactions, simulate typing 'rent' character by character. Assert output: final rows and totals equal a reference brute-force computation. Side-effect assert: the sort comparator is invoked only on the first selector call (spy) across all 4 keystrokes. Performance assert: each keystroke recompute < 25ms. Chaos test: dispatch a store update that prepends 5k new txns mid-typing and assert the sort selector recomputes exactly once and totals stay correct.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Plain useMemo keyed on q recomputes everything each keystroke because q changes every time; without separating the q-independent sort and collapsing four passes into one, the 30k-row work still runs per character and the input stays laggy.

title: Replace layout-thrashing scroll handler that reads offsetTop in a loop on a healthcare patient timeline
business_context: A healthcare EHR patient timeline renders 1,200 event nodes. A scroll handler reads each node's offsetTop to highlight the active date section, forcing synchronous layout (reflow) on every scroll event. Scrolling a long chart pegs CPU and stutters at ~12fps; clinicians scrolling during rounds lose their place. INP on scroll-driven nav is 380ms.
constraints: Up to 1,200 timeline events per patient
Must highlight the correct date section as the user scrolls
Accessibility: active section announced to screen readers
Runs inside an Electron-wrapped desktop EHR (Chromium)
Cannot virtualize fully — print/export needs full DOM

before_state.description: Scroll handler loops over all nodes calling getBoundingClientRect/offsetTop, interleaving reads and writes and forcing layout each frame.

issues: Reads offsetTop (layout) then writes classList (style) in the same loop -> forced reflow per node
Runs on every scroll event with no throttle
setActive triggers a React render on each scroll tick

reasoning: Naive fix: throttle onScroll with requestAnimationFrame. Rejected — rAF reduces frequency but each call still does the read-write-read layout thrash over 1,200 nodes, so a single frame can still cost 40ms+. Alternative: precompute offsetTop once and cache. Rejected — section heights change when events expand/collapse, so cached offsets go stale and highlight the wrong date. Chosen: IntersectionObserver with rootMargin to detect the active section without any manual layout reads, updating a ref and only calling setActive when the section actually changes; class toggles done by the observer callback on entries, not a full loop. Cross-layer trace: observer watches section sentinels (UI) -> intersection callback fires off the main scroll path (async) -> active id stored in ref, setActive called only on change (state) -> aria-current updated for screen readers (a11y) -> no offsetTop reads at all (perf) -> tests assert zero forced reflows during scroll.

after_state.description: IntersectionObserver tracks section visibility with no layout reads; active state updates only on change; aria-current drives accessibility.

files.purpose: Uses IntersectionObserver (no layout reads) and updates active section only on change, with aria-current for screen readers.
Memoized node so only the node whose active flag flips re-renders, not all 1,200.

migration_strategy: Ship the IntersectionObserver timeline behind a `timeline_io_scroll` flag
Enable for read-only timeline views first (no expand/collapse) to validate active accuracy
Verify rootMargin highlights the same section the old code did via screenshot diffs
Ramp to interactive timelines, confirming highlight stays correct after expand/collapse
Remove the offsetTop scroll handler and the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture: render a 1,200-event timeline in jsdom with a mocked IntersectionObserver that fires controlled entries. Assert output: setActive called only when the intersecting section changes, not on every entry. Side-effect assert: aria-current is set on exactly one section. Performance assert (Playwright): record a 2s scroll and assert no forced-reflow long task >16ms. Chaos test: expand a section mid-scroll (height change) and assert the observer still reports the correct active section without manual recompute.
what_breaks_naive_approach: rAF-throttling the loop still performs interleaved offsetTop reads and classList writes across 1,200 nodes, forcing a reflow each frame; caching offsets breaks when sections expand/collapse. Only an IntersectionObserver removes the synchronous layout reads entirely.

title: Cut hydration cost on a gaming leaderboard by streaming RSC and isolating the live-rank client island
business_context: A gaming platform's tournament leaderboard (Next.js App Router) serializes a 500-row leaderboard plus 500 player avatars and stat tooltips as one giant Client Component tree. The RSC payload is 1.8MB and hydration blocks the main thread for 2.1s on mid-tier Android. Only the top-of-page 'your live rank' widget needs interactivity; the rest is static until a 10s poll.
constraints: 500 rows, each with avatar + 6 stats + a hover tooltip
Only 'your rank' updates between 10s polls
Tooltips must work but can hydrate lazily on first hover
Mobile-first: 60% traffic on sub-$300 Android devices
Leaderboard HTML must be crawlable for tournament SEO

before_state.description: The whole leaderboard is a Client Component with use client at the top, so all 500 rows and tooltips ship JS and hydrate eagerly.

issues: Passes 500 rows into a single Client Component
Forces the entire tree to hydrate
use client at the top marks 500 rows + tooltips as client
Hydration cost scales with row count
Tooltip JS ships for all 500 rows up front

reasoning: Naive fix: wrap rows in React.memo. Rejected — memo doesn't reduce hydration; React still must hydrate 500 client subtrees regardless of memo. Alternative: paginate to 50 rows. Rejected — product requires the full 500-row board visible/scrollable and crawlable. Chosen: keep the leaderboard table as a Server Component (zero hydration), extract only the live-rank header into a small client island that polls, and make tooltips a lazily-hydrated client component loaded on first hover via dynamic import. Cross-layer trace: page Server Component fetches board (data) -> table rendered server-side as static HTML (UI) -> LiveRank client island hydrates alone and polls /api/myrank (state/async) -> Tooltip dynamically imported on first pointerenter (perf) -> SEO HTML intact (data) -> tests assert only the island ships in the client bundle for the route -> rollout by flag.

after_state.description: Server-rendered static table, a tiny LiveRank client island that polls, and tooltips that hydrate lazily on first hover.

files.purpose: Renders the leaderboard table fully on the server with no hydration; only LiveRank is interactive.
The only client island, hydrating in isolation and polling live rank every 10s.
Server-rendered row that defers tooltip interactivity to a lazily-hydrated wrapper.
Loads the tooltip component only after the user first hovers a row, keeping initial hydration to near-zero.

migration_strategy: Extract LiveRank island and server-render the table behind a `rsc_leaderboard` flag
Canary on 5% mobile traffic; measure hydration long-task duration and INP
Verify tooltips still appear on hover (lazy) and SEO HTML still contains all rows
Ramp to 100% once hydration long task < 200ms on the Android baseline
Delete the monolithic client Leaderboard and the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture: build the route and inspect the client chunk; assert it contains LiveRank but not the row/tooltip render path at load. Runtime: render Page, assert server HTML includes all 500 rows (SEO) and zero client components hydrate except LiveRank. Side-effect assert: hovering a row triggers exactly one dynamic import of Tooltip. Chaos test: /api/myrank times out; assert LiveRank keeps the last rank and retries on the next interval without crashing the page.
what_breaks_naive_approach: memo and pagination don't change the fact that a top-level use client forces React to hydrate all 500 row subtrees; the 2.1s main-thread block persists. Only removing client-ness from the static rows eliminates the hydration cost.

title: Stabilize callback identity so a chat app's 800-message list stops re-rendering on every incoming message
business_context: A B2B support chat (React) renders a virtualized-by-mistake-but-actually-full list of up to 800 messages. The parent passes inline onReact/onReply callbacks to each MessageRow; every new message recreates these callbacks, so all 800 rows re-render. Agents handling 5 concurrent chats see typing lag and dropped scroll position. INP spikes to 350ms per inbound message.
constraints: Up to 800 messages per conversation in the DOM
New messages arrive via WebSocket up to 4/sec during busy chats
Reactions/replies must still target the correct message id
Scroll position must be preserved on new inbound messages
React 18 with strict mode enabled in dev

before_state.description: Parent recreates inline arrow callbacks each render and passes them to every row, defeating row memoization.

issues: Inline onReact/onReply recreated for all 800 rows every render
MessageRow memo is useless because callback props change identity
Typing in the draft input re-renders the whole list
memo provides no benefit because parent passes fresh callbacks
Re-renders on every parent render despite identical msg

reasoning: Naive fix: useCallback for onReact/onReply in the parent. Rejected — the callbacks close over m.id which differs per row, so you'd need 800 stable callbacks; a single useCallback can't capture per-row id without recreating. Alternative: pass the whole send and let rows build their own handlers. Rejected — rows would then need m.id and rebuild closures internally, still new identity each render unless memoized there. Chosen: pass a single stable dispatch plus the row's own id, and have MessageRow build its handlers with useCallback keyed on [dispatch, msg.id]; lift the draft input into its own component so typing doesn't render the list. Cross-layer trace: parent holds a stable dispatch via useCallback([])(or store dispatch) (state) -> MessageRow receives dispatch + id and memoizes handlers locally (UI) -> draft input isolated so typing is local (perf) -> WS message append changes only the new row's identity (data) -> scroll anchored via a ref (interaction) -> tests assert one inbound message renders one new row.

after_state.description: A single stable dispatch is passed; each memoized row derives its own stable handlers from [dispatch, id]; the draft input is isolated.

files.purpose: Passes one stable dispatch to all rows and isolates the input into Composer so typing no longer renders the list.
Derives per-row handlers from a stable dispatch + id so memo actually prevents re-render of untouched rows.
Owns draft state locally so keystrokes re-render only the composer, never the 800-message list.

migration_strategy: Introduce stable dispatch + Composer behind a `chat_stable_callbacks` flag
Enable for internal agent accounts first; watch INP per inbound message
Verify reactions/replies still hit the correct message id via event logs
Ramp to all agents once INP p95 < 100ms during 4/sec bursts
Remove inline callbacks and the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture: render 800 messages with a per-row render counter; append one new message via the WS mock. Assert output: exactly one new row renders, the other 800 do not. Side-effect assert: typing 5 chars in Composer causes 0 MessageRow renders. Correctness assert: clicking react on row 412 dispatches { type:'react', id: '412' }. Chaos test: append 20 messages in 1s and assert total row renders == 20 (one per new message), scroll offset preserved within 2px.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A single useCallback can't produce 800 distinct per-row handlers, and leaving the draft input in the parent re-renders the whole list on every keystroke; memo stays defeated until both callback identity and input isolation are fixed.

title: Offload a CPU-bound CSV parse to a Web Worker so an IoT telemetry uploader stops freezing the UI for 6 seconds
business_context: An IoT device console lets operators upload a 40MB CSV of sensor readings (up to 1.2M rows). Parsing and validating it happens synchronously on the main thread, freezing the entire SPA for 6s — no spinner animates, clicks queue, and Chrome shows 'page unresponsive'. Operators upload mid-shift and assume the app crashed, re-uploading and doubling backend load.
constraints: CSV up to 40MB / 1.2M rows
Must show parse progress and remain cancelable
Validation rules (range checks, dedupe) must match the server's
Result feeds a chart that expects a typed structure
No new build tooling beyond what Vite already provides

before_state.description: The file is read and parsed row-by-row synchronously in the click handler, blocking the main thread until done.

issues: parseCsv runs synchronously on the main thread
setStatus('parsing') never paints because the thread is blocked
No cancellation or progress
text.split allocates a 1.2M element array up front
Whole parse is one uninterruptible synchronous loop

reasoning: Naive fix: wrap parseCsv in setTimeout/await microtask. Rejected — yielding once doesn't help; the 6s loop still runs in one task and blocks. Alternative: chunk the parse with await between batches on the main thread. Rejected — it unblocks paint but parsing 1.2M rows still steals main-thread time, jank during the whole upload. Chosen: move parsing into a Web Worker that streams the file via ReadableStream, posts progress every N rows, and transfers the result as a typed array; the UI stays fully interactive and shows real progress with cancel. Cross-layer trace: UI posts the File to a worker (async) -> worker reads via stream + TextDecoder incrementally (compute) -> validation rules shared from a common module imported in the worker (config) -> progress messages drive a determinate bar (UI) -> result transferred back (perf) -> AbortController terminates the worker on cancel (interaction) -> tests assert main thread never blocks >16ms.

after_state.description: A Web Worker streams and parses the CSV with shared validation, posting progress; the main thread stays responsive and cancelable.

files.purpose: Streams the file off the main thread, validates per line with shared rules, and reports progress every 50k rows.
Single source of truth for validation/range rules, importable by both the worker and (if needed) server.
Drives the worker, shows determinate progress, and terminates it on cancel so the UI never freezes.

migration_strategy: Add the worker path behind a `csv_worker_parse` flag, keep sync path as fallback
Enable for files >5MB first, where the freeze is worst
Verify parsed output equals the sync parser on a golden fixture set
Ramp to all uploads once main-thread long tasks during parse drop below 50ms
Remove the synchronous parseCsv and the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture: a 1.2M-row CSV blob including malformed and out-of-range rows. Assert output: worker result equals the reference parser (same count, same dropped rows). Side-effect assert: at least 24 progress messages emitted for 1.2M rows. Performance assert (Playwright): during parse, a CSS spinner keeps animating (frame timestamps advance) proving the main thread is free. Chaos test: click Cancel mid-parse and assert worker.terminate is called and no onData fires.
what_breaks_naive_approach: setTimeout or single-yield wrapping still executes the 6s loop in one task; even main-thread chunking steals frame budget for the whole upload. Only a worker keeps the UI thread free, which is why the spinner/cancel work.

title: Defer and group expensive state updates with useTransition so a search-as-you-type results page stays responsive
business_context: A travel B2B SaaS hotel-search page renders a heavy results list (300 cards with maps and price calendars) that updates as the user types filters. Each keystroke synchronously recomputes and re-renders the heavy list, so the filter input drops characters and stutters. Agents filtering on behalf of clients abandon mid-search; INP measured at 480ms.
constraints: Up to 300 result cards, each with a mini price-calendar
Filter input must remain perfectly responsive (no dropped keys)
Results may lag slightly behind input but must converge
React 18 concurrent features available
Must not show a flash of empty results during typing

before_state.description: Filter text and the derived heavy list update in the same synchronous render, so typing blocks on rendering 300 cards.

issues: setQuery triggers a synchronous re-render of 300 heavy ResultCards
Input value and heavy list share one urgent update
No way for React to interrupt the heavy render for the next keystroke

reasoning: Naive fix: debounce setQuery by 300ms. Rejected — debounce makes results feel sluggish and still renders all 300 synchronously when it fires, plus it harms perceived responsiveness for agents who type and expect immediate narrowing. Alternative: virtualize the result grid. Rejected — valid and complementary, but the core issue is that the urgent input update is coupled to the heavy list render; virtualization alone still re-renders visible heavy cards synchronously on each key. Chosen: split state into an urgent input value and a deferred query via useTransition, so React keeps the input update high-priority and renders the heavy list at low priority, interruptible by the next keystroke; show isPending styling instead of a blank flash. Cross-layer trace: input onChange sets urgent value immediately (UI) -> startTransition defers the deferredQuery used by the memo (state) -> heavy list renders as a low-priority transition (concurrency) -> isPending dims the grid instead of clearing it (UX) -> no dropped keys (perf) -> tests assert input stays at full fidelity under fast typing.

after_state.description: Input value is urgent; the heavy filtered list is computed from a transition-deferred query so React can interrupt it for new keystrokes.

files.purpose: Separates urgent input state from the deferred query so React renders the heavy grid at interruptible low priority.
Memoized card so unchanged hotels skip re-render even while the transition re-evaluates the list.

migration_strategy: Wrap the query update in startTransition behind a `search_transition` flag
Canary on 10% of search sessions; measure INP and dropped-key rate
Confirm results still converge to the final query (no stuck-pending)
Ramp to 100% once INP p75 < 200ms and no dropped keys observed
Remove the synchronous setQuery path and the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture: 300 hotels, simulate typing 'paris' at 50ms/char. Assert output: input value reflects every character with no loss (controlled value matches typed string). Side-effect assert: isPending is true during heavy renders and the grid is dimmed, never emptied. Performance assert: keystroke-to-input-paint < 30ms while list renders. Chaos test: type then immediately backspace rapidly and assert the final results match the final query (no stale transition wins).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Debounce delays results and still renders 300 cards synchronously when it fires, so a burst of keystrokes during the render still drops keys; coupling the urgent input to the heavy render is the root cause that only useTransition's priority split fixes.

title: Fix Next.js Image LCP regression by setting priority and sizes on the above-the-fold hero
business_context: A DTC e-commerce homepage migrated to next/image but the hero banner LCP went from 1.9s to 3.6s on mobile. The hero uses fill without sizes (so the browser downloads a 2400px image on a 390px phone) and lacks priority (so it lazy-loads behind below-fold images). LCP failing Core Web Vitals dropped the page out of Google's 'good' bucket, hitting organic traffic.
constraints: Hero must be the LCP element under 2.5s on mobile 4G
Image served from an external CDN allowed in next.config images.remotePatterns
Below-fold images must stay lazy to save bandwidth
No CLS: hero must reserve its aspect ratio
Build must stay on Next 14 App Router

before_state.description: The hero uses fill without a sizes attribute and without priority, so the browser fetches a full-resolution image lazily.

issues: fill without sizes downloads the largest srcset candidate (~2400px) on mobile
No priority: the LCP hero is lazy-loaded after below-fold images
No defined aspect-ratio container risks CLS
No deviceSizes tuning; default candidates may over-serve
No image format hints

reasoning: Naive fix: set loading='eager' on the hero. Rejected — eager loads it without preload priority and still serves the wrong resolution because sizes is missing, so bytes stay huge. Alternative: hardcode a small fixed-width src. Rejected — breaks responsive sharpness on tablets/desktop and reintroduces manual srcset management. Chosen: add priority (which injects a preload link and disables lazy), provide an accurate sizes string so the CDN serves a viewport-appropriate candidate, and wrap the hero in an aspect-ratio container to prevent CLS; tune deviceSizes/formats in config. Cross-layer trace: page marks hero priority (UI) -> Next injects <link rel=preload> with the responsive srcset (network) -> sizes tells the browser to pick the 390px candidate on mobile (bytes) -> aspect-ratio box reserves space (CLS) -> config enables AVIF/WebP (compression) -> tests assert preload present and chosen candidate width.

after_state.description: Hero uses priority + accurate sizes inside an aspect-ratio container; config enables modern formats and sane device sizes.

files.purpose: Marks the hero as the priority LCP image with a correct sizes hint and a reserved aspect ratio to avoid CLS.
Serves AVIF/WebP and a device-size ladder that includes small mobile widths, cutting hero bytes on phones.

migration_strategy: Add priority+sizes+aspect-ratio behind a `hero_lcp_fix` flag (env-gated layout)
Deploy to a preview and run Lighthouse mobile to confirm LCP element is the hero
Canary to 10% prod; watch field LCP p75 in CrUX/RUM
Ramp to 100% once lab LCP < 2.5s and no CLS regression
Remove the flag and document the image guidelines
testing_strategy: Fixture: render Home and assert the hero Image emits a <link rel='preload' as='image'> in the head (Next priority behavior) via a server render snapshot. Assert output: the generated srcset includes a <=414px candidate and sizes='100vw'. Side-effect assert: below-fold rail images do NOT get preload. Performance assert (Lighthouse CI): mobile LCP < 2500ms and CLS < 0.05. Chaos test: simulate a slow CDN (throttle) and assert the aspect-ratio box prevents layout shift while the hero loads.
what_breaks_naive_approach: loading='eager' downloads without preload prioritization and still over-serves resolution because sizes is absent; a hardcoded small src kills desktop sharpness. Only priority+sizes makes Next preload the right-sized candidate as the LCP resource.

title: Break a render-blocking font + CSS chain causing a 2.4s flash on a SaaS auth page
business_context: A B2B SaaS login page has a 2.4s first-contentful-paint delay: a 600KB global CSS bundle and four web-font weights load synchronously in the head, blocking render. New users on the signup flow see a blank white screen; the marketing team measured a 9% signup drop correlated with the slow auth page on cold loads.
constraints: Auth page must visually match the design-system tokens
Cannot break other pages that import the same global CSS
Fonts licensed; must self-host, no Google Fonts CDN
FOUT acceptable; FOIT (invisible text) is not
Build is Vite + React Router (not Next)

before_state.description: A monolithic global CSS and all font weights load render-blocking in the head; the auth page only needs a fraction of them.

issues: global.css (600KB) is render-blocking for every page including auth
all-weights.css loads 4 font weights the auth page doesn't use
No font-display, so text is invisible (FOIT) until fonts load
No font-display: swap (FOIT)
Loads 4 weights when auth uses only 400/600
No preload of the critical weight

reasoning: Naive fix: minify global.css. Rejected — minification shaves a little but the 600KB is mostly unused-on-auth rules; it stays render-blocking. Alternative: inline all CSS into the HTML. Rejected — inlining 600KB bloats the HTML and still blocks parse. Chosen: extract a small critical CSS for the auth route, load the full global.css non-blocking (preload+onload swap), add font-display: swap, preload only the 400 weight used above the fold, and code-split route CSS so each route ships only its styles. Cross-layer trace: HTML inlines critical auth CSS (render) -> full global.css loaded async via rel=preload onload (network) -> font-display swap shows fallback then swaps (text) -> preload critical 400 weight (font) -> Vite per-route CSS chunks (build) -> tests assert FCP element paints before global.css.

after_state.description: Critical auth CSS is inlined; global CSS loads async; fonts use display:swap with the critical weight preloaded; route CSS is split.

files.purpose: Inlines minimal auth styles so text paints immediately while the full stylesheet loads non-render-blocking.

files.content:
@font-face { font-family: 'Inter'; font-weight: 400; font-display: swap; src: url('/fonts/inter-400.woff2') format('woff2'); }
@font-face { font-family: 'Inter'; font-weight: 600; font-display: swap; src: url('/fonts/inter-600.woff2') format('woff2'); }
files.purpose: Loads only the two weights the auth page uses, with font-display:swap to avoid invisible text (FOIT).
Enables per-route CSS code splitting so each route ships only the styles it imports.

migration_strategy: Inline critical auth CSS + async global load behind a build flag, gated to the auth route bundle
Deploy to staging; verify FCP and no FOUT regression via WebPageTest filmstrip
Canary to 10% of auth traffic; watch FCP p75 and signup conversion
Ramp to 100% once FCP < 1.0s on the auth page
Apply the same pattern to other top routes and remove the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture: WebPageTest/Lighthouse run on the auth route. Assert output: FCP < 1.0s and no render-blocking resource flagged for global.css. Side-effect assert: only inter-400/600 fonts are requested on the auth page (network log), not 4 weights. Visual assert: no FOIT — text is visible in a fallback font within 100ms. Chaos test: block the font request entirely and assert text still renders in the system fallback (swap), proving no invisible-text dependency.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Minifying or inlining the full 600KB global CSS keeps it render-blocking or bloats the HTML; without font-display:swap the page still FOITs. Only critical-CSS extraction + async load + swap removes the blocking chain.

title: Stop a Nuxt 3 dashboard from double-fetching on hydration by using useAsyncData payload transfer
business_context: A Nuxt 3 analytics dashboard fetches KPIs in onMounted, so data is fetched once on the server (for SSR) and again on the client after hydration — every page load hits the KPI API twice. The KPI service is rate-limited; the double traffic causes 429s during peak, and users see a content flash as the client refetch overwrites SSR data. API cost doubled.
constraints: KPI API is rate-limited to 50 req/s shared across all users
SSR must keep delivering the KPIs in the initial HTML for SEO/speed
No content flash between SSR and hydration
Auth token is in an httpOnly cookie (server) and a header (client)
Must work with Nuxt 3 SSR + payload extraction

before_state.description: Data is fetched in onMounted, which only runs client-side, but the page also fetches server-side in setup, duplicating requests and causing a flash.

files.content:
<script setup lang="ts">
import { ref, onMounted } from 'vue';
const kpis = ref<Kpi[]>([]);
// server fetch for SSR
const { data } = await useFetch('/api/kpis');
kpis.value = data.value ?? [];
// client refetch (bug)
onMounted(async () => {
  kpis.value = await $fetch('/api/kpis');
});
</script>
<template><KpiGrid :kpis="kpis" /></template>
issues: onMounted refetches the same data the server already fetched
Client refetch overwrites SSR data causing a visible flash
Doubles load on a rate-limited API

reasoning: Naive fix: delete the onMounted refetch. Rejected — that's directionally right but loses any intended client refresh and doesn't address payload transfer or revalidation; we need a structured approach. Alternative: use useFetch with server:false. Rejected — that disables SSR fetching, hurting SEO/LCP and putting all load on the client. Chosen: use useAsyncData/useFetch with a stable key so Nuxt serializes the server result into the payload and hydration reuses it (no client refetch), plus an explicit refresh() bound to a manual refresh button for intentional updates; auth handled via useRequestHeaders on the server. Cross-layer trace: setup runs useFetch on the server (data) -> Nuxt embeds result in the payload keyed by 'kpis' (transfer) -> client hydration reads the payload, no network call (perf) -> auth cookie forwarded server-side via useRequestHeaders (auth) -> manual refresh() triggers a deliberate client fetch (interaction) -> tests assert exactly one fetch per load.

after_state.description: useAsyncData with a key transfers the SSR result via payload; the client reuses it with zero refetch; refresh is explicit and auth-aware.

files.purpose: Fetches KPIs once on the server, transfers via payload keyed 'kpis', and only refetches on explicit refresh().
Server route forwards the auth cookie to the upstream KPI API so the single SSR fetch is authenticated.

migration_strategy: Replace onMounted refetch with keyed useAsyncData behind a `nuxt_payload_kpis` flag
Deploy to staging; assert via server logs exactly one KPI fetch per page load
Canary 10%; watch 429 rate on the KPI API and content-flash reports
Ramp to 100% once duplicate-fetch rate is zero in logs
Remove the onMounted path and the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture: SSR-render dashboard with a mocked KPI upstream and a request counter. Assert output: HTML contains KPI values (SSR) and the hydrated client makes 0 additional KPI calls. Side-effect assert: upstream called exactly once per page load; auth cookie present on that call. Negative test: clicking Refresh triggers exactly one deliberate fetch with the client header. Chaos test: upstream returns 429 on refresh; assert pending resets and prior KPIs remain shown (no wipe).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Just deleting onMounted loses intentional refresh and doesn't fix payload transfer; server:false offloads everything to the client, hurting SSR. Only keyed useAsyncData serializes the server result so hydration reuses it without a second request.

title: Tame a React-Query refetch storm that re-renders a fintech portfolio grid 12 times per price tick
business_context: A wealth-management portfolio grid uses React Query with refetchInterval polling 40 holdings, but invalidateQueries is called on every WebSocket price tick (up to 12/sec), triggering a refetch + re-render of the entire grid each time. The grid janks and the backend sees a refetch storm — 12x the intended poll load. Advisors during market hours see the grid freeze for ~150ms repeatedly.
constraints: 40 holdings, prices tick up to 12/sec via WebSocket
Backend portfolio endpoint must not be hit more than once per 5s
Grid totals must reflect the latest tick within 1s
Auth via bearer token refreshed every 15 min
React Query v5 already adopted

before_state.description: Each WS tick calls invalidateQueries, forcing a network refetch and a full grid re-render instead of locally updating cached prices.

issues: invalidateQueries on every tick forces a network refetch
12 refetches/sec hammer the backend and re-render the whole grid
Prices already in the tick are thrown away in favor of a server round-trip

reasoning: Naive fix: throttle invalidateQueries to 1/sec. Rejected — it still does a full network refetch and full-grid re-render once a second, and prices lag the tick. Alternative: keep a separate React Query key per holding. Rejected — 40 queries multiply subscription overhead and still refetch on invalidation. Chosen: stop invalidating on ticks; instead use setQueryData to patch the cached holding's price in place (immutable update of just that row), keeping the 5s refetchInterval only as a reconciliation safety net; rows memoized so only the ticked holding re-renders. Cross-layer trace: WS tick patches cache via setQueryData updater (data) -> only the affected holding object identity changes (state) -> memoized Row re-renders for that holding only (UI) -> refetchInterval still reconciles drift every 5s (consistency) -> auth token attached in queryFn (auth) -> tests assert zero network refetches on ticks.

after_state.description: Ticks patch the cached price via setQueryData (no refetch); only the affected row re-renders; the 5s interval reconciles.

files.purpose: Applies price ticks directly to the cache so only the ticked holding changes, with no network refetch.
Memoized rows so a single-symbol cache patch re-renders just that row, not all 40.
Authenticated reconciliation fetch used only by the 5s interval, not by ticks.

migration_strategy: Swap invalidate for setQueryData patching behind a `portfolio_tick_patch` flag
Enable for one advisor pod; verify backend refetch rate drops to ~1/5s
Confirm totals match the legacy invalidation path within tolerance
Ramp to all advisors once grid jank disappears (no >50ms tasks on ticks)
Remove the invalidate-on-tick code and the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture: seed a 40-holding portfolio, emit 60 ticks/5s for the same symbol. Assert output: that holding's price updates each tick; totals recompute. Side-effect assert: zero calls to fetchPortfolio during the tick burst (network spy), only one at the 5s boundary. Render assert: only the ticked Row re-renders. Chaos test: a tick arrives for an unknown symbol; assert setQueryData returns prev unchanged (no crash) and no row re-renders.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Throttling invalidation still performs a full network refetch and full-grid render at the throttle rate, and discards the tick's own price in favor of a slower server value; only in-place cache patching avoids the refetch and the fan-out render.

title: Virtualize and windowed-render an Angular CDK table of 100k log lines that crashes the tab
business_context: A devtools log viewer (Angular) renders up to 100,000 log lines into a *ngFor table. The browser tab consumes 2.3GB and crashes on large traces; even at 30k lines, scrolling is at 4fps. SREs investigating incidents lose their session when the tab OOMs, forcing them to re-download multi-hundred-MB logs.
constraints: Up to 100,000 log lines, variable height (wrapped messages)
Must support 'jump to timestamp' and text search highlight
Angular 16 with @angular/cdk already a dependency
Memory must stay under 500MB for a 100k-line trace
Keyboard navigation (j/k, g/G) must work like less

before_state.description: All lines are rendered with *ngFor; the DOM holds 100k nodes, exhausting memory and stalling scroll.

issues: *ngFor renders all 100k lines into the DOM
No trackBy: change detection re-renders everything on any update
Variable-height lines make naive windowing hard but full render is fatal

reasoning: Naive fix: add trackBy to *ngFor. Rejected — trackBy avoids node recreation but still mounts 100k nodes initially, so the OOM and 4fps remain. Alternative: paginate to 1k lines per page. Rejected — SREs scroll continuously through traces; pagination breaks the 'scroll the whole log' workflow and jump-to-timestamp. Chosen: CDK virtual scroll with an autosize strategy for variable-height lines, OnPush change detection, and a trackBy on line id; jump-to-timestamp via scrollToIndex. Cross-layer trace: cdk-virtual-scroll-viewport renders only visible lines (UI) -> autosize strategy measures variable heights (layout) -> OnPush + trackBy minimize CD (perf) -> data source is a plain array bound to the viewport (data) -> scrollToIndex implements jump (interaction) -> keyboard handlers move the index (interaction) -> tests assert DOM node count stays bounded.

after_state.description: CDK virtual scroll renders only visible lines with autosize for variable heights; OnPush + trackBy keep change detection cheap; jump uses scrollToIndex.

files.purpose: Renders only visible variable-height lines via CDK autosize virtual scroll with OnPush and trackBy, plus scrollToIndex jump.
Implements less-style j/k/g/G navigation against the virtual viewport without rendering offscreen lines.

migration_strategy: Introduce the virtual-scroll viewer behind a `log_virtual_scroll` feature toggle
Enable for traces > 5k lines first where OOM risk is highest
Verify search highlight and jump-to-timestamp match the old viewer
Ramp to all traces once memory for 100k lines stays < 500MB
Remove the *ngFor viewer and the toggle
testing_strategy: Fixture: load a 100k-line trace into the component in a Karma/Playwright test. Assert output: rendered .line node count stays under ~60 regardless of total lines. Side-effect assert: tab memory (performance.memory or Playwright metrics) stays under 500MB. Interaction assert: jumpTo(50000) scrolls and renders the correct timestamp window. Chaos test: append 10k lines while scrolled mid-trace and assert no full re-render (node count unchanged) and scroll anchor preserved.
what_breaks_naive_approach: trackBy avoids re-creating nodes but still mounts 100k DOM nodes initially, so the tab still OOMs; pagination breaks continuous scroll and jump-to-timestamp. Only virtualization keeps the live node count bounded.

title: Eliminate a Suspense waterfall in Next.js by hoisting parallel data fetches above nested boundaries
business_context: A social profile page (Next.js App Router) nests three async Server Components — header, posts, and followers — each awaiting its own fetch, but they're nested so they resolve sequentially: header (300ms) -> posts (500ms) -> followers (250ms) = 1.05s blocking. The profile feels slow; users perceive the followers count appearing last. With parallelization the page could resolve in ~500ms.
constraints: Three independent data sources, no inter-dependency
Each section should stream in as it's ready (progressive)
Must preserve per-section error boundaries
Auth cookie required by all three fetches
Posts is the heaviest and should not block header/followers

before_state.description: Async Server Components are nested so each awaits before the next renders, serializing three independent fetches.

issues: Sequential nesting forces header->posts->followers ordering
Total time is the sum of all three fetches
No streaming: nothing shows until the whole chain resolves
Awaits its own fetch then renders Posts, which awaits, then Followers
Couples three independent sections into one sequential chain

reasoning: Naive fix: add Promise.all inside one component fetching all three. Rejected — it parallelizes but loses streaming and per-section error boundaries; a single slow fetch blocks the whole render. Alternative: client-side fetch all three in parallel. Rejected — gives up SSR/streaming and adds hydration cost. Chosen: flatten the tree so the page renders the three sections as siblings, each wrapped in its own Suspense boundary; kick off all fetches in parallel (they start when their component renders, which is concurrently) and let React stream each in as it resolves; per-section ErrorBoundary preserved. Cross-layer trace: page renders Header/Posts/Followers as siblings (UI) -> each async component starts its fetch immediately and concurrently (data) -> Suspense boundaries stream each section's HTML as ready (network) -> auth cookie read per fetch (auth) -> ErrorBoundary isolates failures (resilience) -> tests assert total time ~= max(fetch), not sum.

after_state.description: Sections are siblings, each in its own Suspense boundary; their fetches run concurrently and stream independently.

files.purpose: Renders the three sections as siblings so their fetches start concurrently and each streams in under its own boundary.
Self-contained header that fetches only its own data with the session cookie, no nested children.
Per-section error boundary so one failed fetch degrades only its section, not the whole profile.

migration_strategy: Flatten the component tree behind a `profile_parallel_sections` flag
Deploy to staging; measure server render TTFB and section stream timings
Canary 10%; confirm followers no longer appears last and total time ~= max fetch
Ramp to 100% once profile p75 render < 600ms
Remove the nested chain and the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture: mock the three fetches with delays 300/500/250ms. Assert output: total server render completes near 500ms (max), not 1050ms (sum) — measured via timing. Side-effect assert: each fetch is invoked once with the session cookie. Streaming assert: header and followers stream before posts (independent). Chaos test: make the posts fetch reject; assert only the Posts section shows the error boundary while header and followers render normally.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A single Promise.all component parallelizes but blocks the entire render on the slowest fetch and loses per-section streaming and isolated error boundaries; nesting serializes the fetches. Sibling Suspense boundaries give both parallelism and progressive streaming.

title: Replace deep prop-drilling re-renders with Zustand selectors on a collaborative whiteboard toolbar
business_context: A collaborative whiteboard (React) keeps the entire app state (200+ shapes, selection, tool, zoom) in one top-level useReducer prop-drilled through 8 layers. Selecting a shape re-renders the whole canvas plus the 30-button toolbar, dropping drawing latency to 90ms per pointer move. With 5 collaborators, cursor updates compound the re-render storm.
constraints: Up to 200 shapes; pointer events fire at ~120Hz while drawing
Toolbar (30 buttons) must reflect active tool but not re-render on shape edits
Collaborator cursors update independently of local shape edits
Undo/redo must keep working (state history)
No prop API change for leaf components consumed by a plugin SDK

before_state.description: One reducer state is drilled everywhere; any dispatch re-renders the whole tree because the context value changes.

issues: Whole state passed to Toolbar and Canvas
Any dispatch re-renders both even if unrelated
Pointer-move shape edits re-render the 30-button toolbar
Reads whole state to know active tool
Re-renders all 30 buttons on every shape edit

reasoning: Naive fix: wrap Toolbar/Canvas in React.memo. Rejected — both receive the whole state object which changes identity on every dispatch, so memo never short-circuits. Alternative: split into multiple contexts (tool, shapes, cursors). Rejected — workable but context still re-renders all consumers of a given slice; the toolbar would still re-render on any tool-context change and we'd thread many providers. Chosen: move state into a Zustand store and have each component subscribe to only the slice it needs via selectors with shallow equality; pointer-move updates only the shapes slice, so the toolbar (subscribed to tool) doesn't re-render; cursors live in their own slice. Cross-layer trace: store holds shapes/tool/cursors/history (state) -> Toolbar subscribes to s.tool only (UI) -> Canvas subscribes to s.shapes; cursor layer subscribes to s.cursors (UI) -> pointer-move action mutates shapes immutably (data) -> undo/redo via a history middleware (feature) -> leaf prop API preserved by passing resolved values (compat) -> tests assert toolbar doesn't render on shape edits.

after_state.description: A Zustand store with sliced selectors; each component subscribes only to its slice, so shape edits don't touch the toolbar or cursor layer.

files.purpose: Centralizes state in slices with built-in undo/redo history so subscribers can pick only what they need.
Each tool button subscribes only to its own active boolean, so shape edits never re-render the toolbar.
Subscribes to the cursors slice with shallow equality so collaborator cursor moves don't re-render shapes.

migration_strategy: Introduce the Zustand store and slice-subscribe Toolbar/CursorLayer behind a `whiteboard_zustand` flag
Keep the reducer path for instant rollback during the canary
Enable for single-user boards first; verify undo/redo parity
Ramp to multiplayer boards once pointer-move latency p95 < 16ms
Remove the reducer + prop drilling and the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture: render App with a render counter on Toolbar and CursorLayer; dispatch 100 shape updates. Assert output: Toolbar renders 0 times, CursorLayer renders 0 times during shape edits. Side-effect assert: undo after N edits restores the exact prior shapes array (deep equal to history). Interaction assert: setTool re-renders only the two affected tool buttons. Chaos test: 5 simulated collaborator cursor streams at 60Hz; assert the shapes layer never re-renders due to cursor updates.
what_breaks_naive_approach: React.memo can't help because the whole-state object changes identity on every dispatch; even multi-context still re-renders all consumers of the changed slice. Per-component selector subscriptions are what isolate the toolbar and cursors from shape edits.

title: Debounce + abort an autocomplete that fires a request per keystroke and renders stale results
business_context: A SaaS admin search box queries an org-members API on every keystroke (no debounce, no cancellation). Typing 'jonathan' fires 8 requests; out-of-order responses cause results to flicker and sometimes show stale matches for an earlier prefix. The members API team flagged the box as their top traffic source — 6x the necessary calls.
constraints: Members API must receive at most ~3 req/s per user
Results must always reflect the latest query (no stale wins)
Keep keyboard navigation of the dropdown
React 18, fetch only (no extra data lib)
Box appears on 12 admin pages; fix must be reusable

before_state.description: Every keystroke triggers an immediate fetch with no debounce or abort; late responses overwrite newer ones.

issues: One request per keystroke, no debounce
No AbortController: out-of-order responses cause stale results
setResults from a late response can overwrite the latest

reasoning: Naive fix: add a 300ms debounce. Rejected — debounce reduces volume but doesn't prevent the surviving in-flight request from being superseded by a faster late one; stale-result flicker remains under variable latency. Alternative: track a request sequence number and ignore old ones. Rejected — works but still wastes bandwidth on superseded requests. Chosen: a reusable useDebouncedSearch hook that debounces the query AND aborts the prior request via AbortController, so only the latest query's response is applied and superseded requests are canceled. Cross-layer trace: input updates q (UI) -> hook debounces 250ms (timing) -> on fire, aborts the previous controller and starts a new fetch (network) -> aborted responses never call setState (correctness) -> dropdown renders latest results (UI) -> hook reused across 12 pages (DRY) -> tests assert stale responses are ignored.

after_state.description: A reusable hook debounces the query and aborts superseded requests, guaranteeing only the latest response renders.

files.purpose: Reusable debounced+abortable search hook ensuring only the latest query's response updates state.
Uses the shared hook and adds combobox/listbox ARIA so keyboard nav stays intact across all 12 pages.

migration_strategy: Publish useDebouncedSearch and adopt it in MemberSearch behind a `search_debounce_abort` flag
Roll to 2 of 12 admin pages; verify members-API request volume drops ~6x
Confirm no stale-result flicker via manual fast-typing QA
Adopt across all 12 pages once volume and correctness are validated
Remove the per-keystroke fetch and the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture: mock the members API with variable latency (first request slow, second fast). Assert output: only the latest query's results render even when an earlier request resolves later. Side-effect assert: typing 8 chars fires <=3 fetches (debounce) and aborts the in-flight one on each new keystroke (signal.aborted true). Negative test: clearing the box sets results to []. Chaos test: API returns 500 for one query; assert the error propagates without leaving stale results, and the next keystroke recovers.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Debounce alone still lets a faster late response for an older prefix overwrite the newest results under variable latency; a sequence-number guard wastes bandwidth on superseded calls. AbortController cancels the superseded request and prevents its setState.

title: Cut INP on a Kanban board by batching drag-over state into a ref and committing on drop
business_context: A project-management Kanban board (React) updates React state on every dragover event while reordering cards across 6 columns with 400 cards. Each dragover (fired ~60/s) setState triggers a re-render of the dragged column, so dragging feels gluey and INP hits 320ms. Teams reordering sprints abandon drag mid-flight.
constraints: Up to 400 cards across 6 columns
Drag preview must follow the pointer smoothly at 60fps
Drop must persist the new order to the server (optimistic)
Must support keyboard reordering for accessibility
React 18; using native HTML5 drag-and-drop

before_state.description: dragover sets a hovered-index state on every event, re-rendering the column for each of ~60 events per second.

issues: setHoverIdx on every dragover (~60/s) re-renders the column
Re-render cost scales with card count
No optimistic persistence or keyboard support

reasoning: Naive fix: throttle setHoverIdx to 100ms. Rejected — the drop indicator then lags the pointer and feels imprecise; throttling state still re-renders periodically. Alternative: use a drag library like dnd-kit. Rejected — valid long-term but a heavy migration for 6 columns; the immediate fix is to stop re-rendering on dragover. Chosen: store the hovered index in a ref and imperatively toggle a CSS class on the target element via the DOM during dragover (no React render), committing the final order to state only on drop (one render), with optimistic server persist and keyboard reorder via arrow keys. Cross-layer trace: dragover writes ref + toggles class imperatively (UI, no render) -> drop reads ref, computes new order, setState once (state) -> optimistic onReorder posts to server (data/async) -> rollback on failure (resilience) -> keyboard handler reorders via state for a11y (interaction) -> tests assert dragover causes 0 renders.

after_state.description: dragover updates a ref and toggles a class imperatively (no render); drop commits once with optimistic persistence; keyboard reorder supported.

files.purpose: Keeps drag-over feedback off the React render path (ref + imperative class) and commits one state update on drop, with keyboard reorder.
Applies the new order optimistically and rolls back on a failed server persist.

migration_strategy: Land ref-based dragover + optimistic order behind a `kanban_imperative_drag` flag
Enable for boards under 100 cards first; verify drop accuracy vs legacy
Compare INP during drag in RUM on canary vs control
Ramp to all boards once INP p75 < 200ms during drag
Remove the setState-on-dragover path and the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture: render a 400-card column with a render counter; simulate 60 dragover events. Assert output: 0 React renders during dragover; exactly 1 render on drop. Side-effect assert: drop calls persist with the correct reordered id array; a 500 from persist rolls back to the prior order. Interaction assert: ArrowDown swaps a card with its neighbor. Chaos test: persist rejects on drop; assert the UI reverts to the original order and surfaces a retry.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Throttling setState makes the drop indicator lag the pointer and still re-renders periodically; only moving drag-over feedback off the render path (ref + imperative DOM) keeps 60fps while committing a single render on drop.

title: Stop SVG re-render thrash in a real-time IoT gauge dashboard with 60 animated dials
business_context: An industrial IoT monitoring wall shows 60 SVG gauges, each animating a needle to a live sensor value updated 10/sec via MQTT-over-WebSocket. Each update re-renders all 60 React SVG components (thousands of path nodes), pegging a control-room display at 8fps and delaying alarm color changes by ~700ms — unacceptable for safety monitoring.
constraints: 60 gauges, each value updates up to 10/sec
Alarm color must flip within 100ms of threshold crossing
Runs on a fixed control-room PC (mid-range GPU) full-screen 24/7
Memory must stay flat over multi-day uptime (no leak)
Needle motion must be smooth (no snapping)

before_state.description: Each gauge is a React component re-rendering its full SVG on every value change; all 60 update per tick.

issues: Re-renders the entire SVG (31 lines) on every value change
Static tick marks re-created each render
React reconciles all 60 gauges per tick
setValues replaces the whole map, changing identity for all gauges
All 60 gauges re-render per single-sensor update

reasoning: Naive fix: deeper memo / shallow compare per gauge. Rejected — the values map identity changes each tick so even memoized gauges whose value didn't change re-render; and the ones that did change still re-render their whole SVG. Alternative: canvas-render all gauges. Rejected — loses SVG accessibility/styling and is a large rewrite; the static structure is the issue, not the renderer. Chosen: render each gauge's static structure once and animate only the needle and alarm color imperatively via refs (setAttribute on transform/stroke) driven by a per-gauge subscription to an off-React store; no React render on value change. Cross-layer trace: store holds per-id values, notifies per-id listeners (data) -> each Gauge subscribes to its own id and writes needle transform via ref (UI, no render) -> alarm color set imperatively within 100ms (safety) -> static ticks rendered once (perf) -> rAF coalesces multiple MQTT messages per frame (async) -> tests assert React render count is 60 total (mount only).

after_state.description: Static SVG rendered once per gauge; the needle and alarm color are updated imperatively via refs from a per-id store subscription, with rAF coalescing.

files.purpose: Per-id value store that coalesces MQTT bursts into one rAF flush and notifies only the affected gauge.
Renders static structure once and mutates only the needle's transform/stroke imperatively, never re-rendering on value change.
Feeds MQTT values into the store; the React tree never re-renders on updates.

migration_strategy: Ship the imperative gauge + store behind a `gauge_imperative` flag on a test wall
Run side-by-side with the React-render wall; compare fps and alarm latency
Verify alarm color flips within 100ms of threshold in a controlled test
Promote to the production control room once stable over 24h
Remove the React-render Gauge and the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture: mount 60 gauges with a React render counter; push 600 values/sec for 5s. Assert output: total React renders == 60 (mount only), 0 on value updates. Side-effect assert: a value crossing 0.9 sets stroke='red' on that needle within one rAF (<100ms). Memory assert: heap snapshot stable after 1M pushes (listeners not leaking on unmount).
