Chaos test: an MQTT id with no matching gauge is pushed; assert it's ignored without error and doesn't allocate unbounded.
what_breaks_naive_approach: The values-map identity change re-renders all 60 memoized gauges; even per-gauge memo still re-renders the full SVG of changed gauges. Imperative needle updates bypass React reconciliation entirely, which is the only way to hit 60fps with 10Hz x 60 streams.

title: Fix a Remix route that ships a 900KB markdown+highlighter bundle to every blog reader
business_context: A developer-blog (Remix) bundles a markdown renderer and a 700KB syntax-highlighter into the root route, so every reader downloads it even on posts with no code blocks. Mobile readers on the changelog index (no code) wait 3.1s for TTI. Only ~30% of posts contain code, yet 100% pay the highlighter cost.
constraints: Highlighter must still render code blocks correctly when present
Markdown is rendered server-side for SEO
No layout shift when a code block hydrates highlighting
Remix (Vite) build; keep nested routes
Index pages with no code must not load the highlighter at all

before_state.description: The highlighter is statically imported in the post component, so it's in the route bundle regardless of whether a post has code.

issues: highlightAll statically imports a 700KB highlighter into every post bundle
Runs even on posts with no code blocks
Index pages inherit the cost via the shared chunk

reasoning: Naive fix: tree-shake the highlighter. Rejected — it's used (called unconditionally) so tree-shaking can't remove it; the import is reachable. Alternative: highlight server-side at build time. Rejected — strong option but the existing CMS renders markdown at request time and would need a pipeline rewrite; not the targeted fix. Chosen: detect at loader time whether the post contains code; only when it does, dynamically import the highlighter on the client after paint and apply it to <pre> blocks, reserving their height to avoid CLS. Cross-layer trace: loader flags hasCode (data) -> server renders markdown HTML including raw code (SEO) -> client conditionally import()s highlighter only if hasCode (perf) -> highlight applied post-paint without shifting layout (CLS) -> index/no-code posts never load it (bytes) -> tests assert no highlighter chunk on no-code routes.

after_state.description: The loader flags whether code exists; the highlighter is dynamically imported on the client only for posts that contain code, after paint.

files.purpose: Loads the highlighter on the client only when the rendered HTML actually contains code, after first paint.
Scoped highlighter entrypoint placed in its own dynamically-imported chunk so it never lands in the route bundle.
Ensures highlighting only changes color (not metrics), so applying it post-paint causes no layout shift.

migration_strategy: Move highlighter to a dynamic import gated by hasCode behind a `lazy_highlighter` flag
Verify in CI that no-code routes' client bundle excludes the highlighter chunk
Canary 10%; measure TTI on index/no-code posts and highlight correctness on code posts
Ramp to 100% once no-code TTI < 1.5s and code posts still highlight
Remove the static import and the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture: build and assert via the bundle graph that blog.$slug's initial client chunk does not include the highlighter, which lives in an async chunk. Runtime: render a no-code post and assert import('~/lib/highlighter') is never called; render a code post and assert it's called once after mount. CLS assert: highlight a code block and assert its bounding box is unchanged (no shift). Chaos test: highlighter import rejects (network); assert the code still shows as plain text and the page doesn't error.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Tree-shaking can't drop a highlighter that's unconditionally called; build-time highlighting needs a pipeline rewrite. Gating a dynamic import on actual code presence is what keeps the highlighter out of no-code and index bundles.

title: Repair a useEffect dependency bug that re-subscribes a WebSocket on every render, leaking connections
business_context: A live-ops dashboard (React) opens a metrics WebSocket in a useEffect whose dependency array includes an object recreated each render, so the effect tears down and re-opens the socket on every render — dozens of connections per minute. The metrics backend hit its per-IP connection cap, dropping legitimate clients; ops saw 'connection limit exceeded' alerts and flickering charts.
constraints: Metrics backend caps 5 concurrent WS connections per client IP
Socket must reconnect on genuine config change (e.g., switched dashboard)
Must clean up on unmount (no zombie sockets)
Reconnect with backoff on transient drops
React 18 strict mode double-invokes effects in dev

before_state.description: The effect depends on a config object literal recreated each render, so it re-subscribes constantly and leaks sockets.

issues: config is a new object each render, so the effect runs every render
Each run opens a new WebSocket, leaking connections
setData triggers a render, which recreates config, which re-runs the effect

reasoning: Naive fix: remove config from deps (empty array). Rejected — then switching dashboards won't reconnect, showing stale metrics for the wrong dashboard. Alternative: useMemo the config object. Rejected — fixes identity but the effect still closes over filters via config and would need filters in deps anyway; cleaner to depend on primitives. Chosen: depend on the primitive values (dashboardId and a stable serialization of filters) so the effect re-runs only on real changes; add reconnect-with-backoff and ensure cleanup; guard against strict-mode double-invoke with a closed flag. Cross-layer trace: effect deps are [dashboardId, filtersKey] primitives (state) -> socket opens once per real config (network) -> message handler updates data without recreating deps (correctness) -> cleanup closes socket on unmount/change (resource) -> backoff handles transient drops (resilience) -> tests assert one socket per config.

after_state.description: The effect depends on primitive config values and a stable filters key, opening exactly one socket per real change, with backoff reconnect and clean teardown.

files.purpose: Depends on primitive dashboardId + a stable filters key so the socket re-subscribes only on real config changes.
Single socket with exponential backoff reconnect and a closed flag that survives strict-mode double-invoke and guarantees teardown.

migration_strategy: Replace the leaking effect with primitive-dep + socket helper behind a `metrics_ws_fix` flag
Deploy to staging; assert via server WS logs exactly one connection per client per config
Canary 10%; watch the per-IP connection-cap alert disappear
Ramp to 100% once connection counts are stable
Remove the object-dep effect and the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture: render MetricsDash, trigger 20 unrelated re-renders (state changes). Assert output: exactly one WebSocket is created (constructor spy), not 20. Side-effect assert: changing dashboardId closes the old socket and opens one new one. Cleanup assert: unmount calls ws.close once. Chaos test: simulate onclose mid-session and assert backoff reconnect fires with increasing delays and stops after the component unmounts (closed flag respected).
what_breaks_naive_approach: An empty dep array stops the leak but never reconnects on dashboard switch (stale/wrong metrics); useMemo on the object still requires filters handling. Depending on primitive values is what makes the effect fire exactly when config truly changes.

title: Cut a Next.js cart page's server render time by replacing N+1 product fetches with a batched DataLoader
business_context: A Next.js App Router cart page (RSC) renders 25 line items and fetches each product detail in its own await inside the item component, producing 25 sequential upstream calls of ~40ms each — a 1s server render before any HTML streams. During flash sales the product service saw a fan-out of thousands of single-item calls, raising its p99 and cascading timeouts.
constraints: Carts hold up to 50 line items
Product service supports a /batch endpoint (max 100 ids)
Server render must complete under 250ms p95
Auth via signed cookie forwarded to the product service
Must not over-fetch: only ids present in the cart

before_state.description: Each line-item Server Component awaits its own product fetch, serializing N upstream calls per render.

issues: Renders 25 LineItem components each doing its own fetch
No batching of product lookups
getProduct called once per line item (N+1)
Sequential awaits because each component awaits independently

reasoning: Naive fix: Promise.all the product fetches in the page. Rejected — it parallelizes but still issues N separate upstream calls, multiplying load on the product service during sales (the fan-out problem). Alternative: cache getProduct with React cache() so duplicates dedupe. Rejected — dedupes identical ids but doesn't batch distinct ids into one call. Chosen: a per-request DataLoader that collects all product-id reads in a tick and issues a single /batch call, wired via React cache() so the loader instance is request-scoped; LineItem stays simple and awaits loader.load(id). Cross-layer trace: page renders items (UI) -> each LineItem calls loader.load(id) (data) -> DataLoader batches all ids into one /batch call per render (network) -> auth cookie forwarded on the batch call (auth) -> request-scoped via React cache so no cross-request leakage (isolation) -> tests assert one upstream call total.

after_state.description: A request-scoped DataLoader batches all product reads into a single /batch call; line items await loader.load(id).

files.purpose: Request-scoped (via React cache) DataLoader that coalesces all product-id reads in a render into one authenticated /batch call.
Reads its product via the shared loader so all line items' ids batch into a single call automatically.
Unchanged structure; the batching is transparent because line items share the request-scoped loader.

migration_strategy: Introduce the DataLoader and route LineItem through it behind a `cart_batch_products` flag
Shadow-compare batched results vs per-item results for correctness on staging
Canary 10% of carts; watch product-service call count and cart render p95
Ramp to 100% once render p95 < 250ms and upstream fan-out collapses
Remove getProduct per-item calls and the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture: a 25-item cart with a mocked /batch endpoint and a call counter. Assert output: rendered HTML shows correct name/price per line. Side-effect assert: exactly one POST to /batch with all 25 ids, zero single-id calls; the auth cookie is present. Order assert: loader returns products in id order. Chaos test: /batch omits one id (deleted product); assert that line item renders a graceful 'unavailable' placeholder instead of crashing the render.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Promise.all still issues N upstream calls (the sale-time fan-out), and React cache() only dedupes identical ids; neither batches distinct ids. A per-request DataLoader is required to collapse N reads into one /batch call.

title: Stop a video-conferencing grid from re-laying-out all 49 tiles when one participant toggles mute
business_context: A video-conferencing web app (React) renders a 7x7 speaker grid. Mute/camera/raise-hand state lives in one participants array; any participant event (mute toggle, ~20/min in big calls) replaces the array, re-rendering and re-laying-out all 49 video tiles, causing visible video stutter and dropped frames on the active speaker. Users in 50-person all-hands report flickering.
constraints: Up to 49 visible tiles (7x7) plus an overflow strip
Video <video> elements must NOT remount on metadata changes (would re-negotiate streams)
Mute/handraise badges must update within 200ms
Active-speaker reordering still allowed but should not remount tiles
WebRTC streams attached imperatively via srcObject

before_state.description: All participant metadata lives in one array replaced on every event; tiles re-render and the keying causes <video> remounts.

issues: key={i} (index) causes tile/video remounts on reorder
Whole participants array replaced on each event
Mute toggle re-renders all 49 tiles
srcObject re-assigned on every render (effect with no deps)
Re-renders fully on any participant metadata change
Index key remounts the <video> on reorder, re-negotiating the stream

reasoning: Naive fix: key tiles by participant id. Rejected — necessary but not sufficient; the whole array still replaces so all tiles re-render and srcObject re-assigns each time. Alternative: split into one context per participant. Rejected — 49 providers is heavy and reordering still re-renders the grid. Chosen: key tiles by stable participant id, memoize Tile, only assign srcObject when the stream reference actually changes (effect dep on stream), and store volatile badge state (muted/hand) in a per-participant external store so a mute toggle updates only that tile's badge via subscription, not the whole grid. Cross-layer trace: grid maps stable-id tiles (UI) -> Tile memoized; <video> never remounts (media) -> srcObject set only when stream identity changes (resource) -> badges read per-id from a store subscription (state) -> mute event notifies one tile (perf) -> reorder changes order not identity (layout) -> tests assert <video> not remounted on mute.

after_state.description: Tiles keyed by id and memoized; <video> srcObject set only on stream change; badges driven by a per-participant store so mute toggles update one tile.

files.purpose: Holds volatile per-participant badge state so a mute toggle notifies only that participant's tile.
Memoized by id+stream so the <video> never remounts on badge changes; badges come from the per-id store.
Routes roster changes to React (stable id keys) and badge changes to the store, so mute toggles bypass the grid render.

migration_strategy: Split roster vs badge events and adopt id keys + memo behind a `grid_badge_store` flag
Test on small calls first; verify no <video> remount on mute (stream stats stay continuous)
Canary on large all-hands; measure dropped video frames on the active speaker
Ramp to 100% once badge updates render only the target tile
Remove the index-keyed array path and the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture: render a 49-tile grid with a per-tile render + video-remount probe. Assert output: a mute event for tile 17 re-renders only tile 17's badge; the <video> element identity is unchanged (not remounted). Side-effect assert: srcObject is assigned once per tile, not on badge changes. Reorder assert: an active-speaker reorder changes DOM order without remounting any <video>. Chaos test: 20 mute toggles/sec for 10s and assert zero <video> remounts and no full-grid render.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Index keys remount the <video> on reorder (re-negotiating WebRTC), and replacing the whole array re-renders all 49 tiles and re-assigns every srcObject on a single mute toggle. Stable ids + a per-participant badge store isolate badge updates from the media tiles.

title: Stop styled-components from generating new class hashes per render on a 300-row pricing table
business_context: A B2B SaaS pricing comparison table (React + styled-components) defines styled components with props-driven dynamic styles inside the render body, so every render generates new class names and injects fresh <style> rules. Resizing the window or hovering re-creates 300x4 rules, ballooning the <style> tag to 6MB and dropping interaction to 5fps. The CSSOM recalculation is the bottleneck.
constraints: 300 rows x 4 styled cells = 1,200 styled instances
Dynamic styles (highlight tier, currency color) must still work
Cannot drop styled-components (used app-wide, themed)
SSR critical CSS extraction must keep working
Style injection must not grow unbounded over a session

before_state.description: styled components are defined inside the component body, so each render creates new component instances and new injected rules.

issues: styled.td defined inside map/render: new component + class hash each render
Injects 1,200 fresh rules per render, growing the <style> tag
Dynamic values baked into static template, defeating caching

reasoning: Naive fix: memoize the component with useMemo. Rejected — styled components defined in render are recreated even if memoized per row, and the dynamic interpolation still produces unbounded class variants. Alternative: switch to inline style={} objects. Rejected — inline styles can't do pseudo-selectors/media queries used elsewhere and bypass theming. Chosen: hoist the styled component to module scope and move dynamic values into a single style prop / CSS custom properties, so styled-components produces one stable base class plus per-instance CSS variables (no new hashes); SSR extraction unaffected. Cross-layer trace: module-scope styled.td with var() (UI) -> dynamic color/bg passed via style={{ '--c': ..., '--bg': ... }} (data) -> styled produces one cached rule (CSSOM) -> no per-render injection (perf) -> SSR critical CSS still extracts the single rule (SSR) -> tests assert stable class count.

after_state.description: A module-scoped styled component uses CSS custom properties for dynamics, producing one stable class and no per-render rule injection.

files.purpose: Single module-scoped styled component with a stable class; dynamics flow through CSS variables.
Passes per-row dynamics as CSS variables on a stable class, so no new style rules are injected on render.

migration_strategy: Hoist styled definitions + adopt CSS vars behind a `pricing_css_vars` flag
Verify SSR critical-CSS output still contains the single Cell rule
Canary 10%; measure <style> tag size growth and interaction fps
Ramp to 100% once the <style> tag stays flat across a session
Remove the in-render styled definitions and the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture: render the table and re-render 50 times (simulated resizes). Assert output: cells show correct color/bg via computed style. Side-effect assert: the number of injected styled-components classes stays constant across re-renders (read document.styleSheets rule count). Memory assert: <style> tag length does not grow after the first render. Chaos test: rapidly toggle highlightTier 100x and assert no new rules are injected (only CSS variable values change).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Defining styled components in render recreates them and emits new class hashes regardless of memoization; inline styles lose pseudo/media support. Module-scope + CSS variables is the only path that keeps a single cached rule while staying dynamic.

title: Convert a synchronous 4,000-node tree render into an incremental reveal to unblock a CMS page-builder
business_context: A headless-CMS page builder (React) renders a deeply nested component tree (up to 4,000 nodes) representing a marketing page. Opening a large page renders the entire tree synchronously, blocking the editor for 1.8s with a frozen toolbar and a 'page unresponsive' warning. Editors opening templated landing pages lose time on every open.
constraints: Up to 4,000 nodes, arbitrary nesting depth
Editor toolbar must stay interactive during load
Final rendered tree must match the synchronous output exactly
Drag-and-drop of blocks must work once loaded
React 18 available

before_state.description: The whole tree is rendered in one synchronous pass; deep recursion blocks the main thread until complete.

issues: Renders 4,000 nodes in one synchronous commit
No yielding: toolbar frozen until done
Recursive synchronous render of the entire subtree
No prioritization of above-the-fold blocks

reasoning: Naive fix: virtualize the tree. Rejected — arbitrary nesting (not a flat list) makes windowing impractical; a page is a single visual flow, not a scrollable list of equal items. Alternative: render top-level blocks only and lazy-mount children on scroll. Rejected — page-builder needs the full layout measured for drag targets; partial mounting breaks drop zones. Chosen: wrap the render in startTransition so the heavy tree commit is a low-priority, interruptible render keeping the toolbar responsive, and progressively reveal subtrees by chunking children with a Suspense-friendly incremental list (render first N children urgently, defer the rest in a transition). Cross-layer trace: toolbar state stays urgent (UI) -> page tree rendered via startTransition (concurrency) -> BlockView reveals children in chunks deferred across frames (perf) -> identical final DOM (correctness) -> drag targets available after full reveal (interaction) -> tests assert toolbar stays interactive.

after_state.description: The tree renders in a transition (interruptible) and children reveal incrementally in chunks, keeping the toolbar responsive while converging to the same output.

files.purpose: Commits the heavy tree as a transition so the toolbar (urgent updates) stays interactive during the 4,000-node render.
Reveals children in 50-node chunks during idle time so each frame stays short while converging to the full tree.

migration_strategy: Wrap the page render in a transition + chunked reveal behind a `builder_incremental_render` flag
Verify the fully-revealed DOM is identical to the synchronous render via snapshot
Canary on large templated pages; measure toolbar INP during open
Ramp to 100% once open INP < 200ms and full reveal completes < 2s
Remove the synchronous renderer and the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture: a 4,000-node page tree. Assert output: after all idle callbacks flush, the DOM equals the synchronous PageRenderer output (deep snapshot equality). Side-effect assert: during the transition, a toolbar button click handler still fires within 50ms (interactivity probe). Performance assert: no single render commit exceeds 16ms. Chaos test: switch to a different page mid-reveal and assert the in-flight reveal is abandoned cleanly (no leftover partial DOM from the old page).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Virtualization assumes a flat scrollable list, which a nested page is not; lazy-on-scroll mounting breaks drag-and-drop drop zones that need the full layout. A transition + chunked reveal yields the same DOM while keeping the toolbar responsive.

title: Eliminate hydration mismatch + double render on a localized Next.js storefront using a date in initial state
business_context: A global storefront (Next.js App Router) renders a 'deals end in' countdown that initializes state with new Date() and toLocaleString() during SSR, then the client computes a different value, causing a hydration mismatch. React discards the server HTML and re-renders the whole product section on the client — a 600ms re-render plus a flash of the wrong time, and console errors flooding Sentry (12k/day).
constraints: Countdown must show server-authoritative deal end time
Locale/timezone formatting must match the user's
No hydration mismatch warnings (Sentry budget)
SSR HTML must be cacheable at the edge per-locale
React 18 strict hydration

before_state.description: Components compute time-dependent values during render on both server and client, producing divergent HTML and a hydration mismatch.

issues: new Date() in initial state differs between server render and client hydration
toLocaleTimeString uses server locale on SSR, user locale on client
Mismatch causes React to throw away server HTML and re-render

reasoning: Naive fix: suppressHydrationWarning on the span. Rejected — it silences the warning but the values still differ, so React still re-renders the subtree client-side and users see a flash; it hides the bug, not fixes it. Alternative: render the countdown client-only with a loading placeholder. Rejected — loses SSR for the deal time (SEO/LCP) and shows a placeholder flash. Chosen: render a stable server value (formatted end time, not 'now'), and start the live ticking only after mount via useEffect so the server and first client render are identical; do locale formatting with a fixed timezone passed from the server. Cross-layer trace: server passes endsAt + locale (data) -> first render shows a deterministic formatted remaining computed from a server 'now' passed as prop (SSR) -> useEffect starts the interval post-hydration to tick (client) -> identical initial HTML => no mismatch (correctness) -> edge cache stays valid per locale (cache) -> tests assert server and client first render match.

after_state.description: The first render uses server-provided time so SSR and hydration match exactly; ticking starts only after mount; locale/timezone come from the server.

files.purpose: Seeds state with the server's now so the first render matches; live ticking begins only after hydration.
Deterministic, locale/timezone-stable formatting so server and client produce identical strings.
Passes a single server timestamp + locale/timezone so the client's first render is deterministic and cacheable per locale.

migration_strategy: Pass serverNow + locale and defer ticking behind a `countdown_ssr_safe` flag
Deploy to a preview; confirm zero hydration warnings in the console
Canary 10%; watch Sentry hydration-error volume drop
Ramp to 100% once mismatch errors hit zero and no time flash observed
Remove the new Date()-in-render path and the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture: server-render Countdown then hydrate with the same serverNow. Assert output: server HTML string equals the client's first render output (no mismatch). Side-effect assert: setInterval starts only after mount (not during SSR). Negative test: an already-expired deal renders 00:00:00 without going negative. Chaos test: run hydration with React's strict error logging and assert no 'Text content did not match' warning is emitted.
what_breaks_naive_approach: suppressHydrationWarning hides the warning but React still re-renders the subtree because the values differ, causing the flash; client-only rendering loses SSR. Seeding state with a server timestamp makes the first render deterministic, which is the actual fix.

title: Memoize an expensive geospatial clustering computation re-run on every map pan in a logistics console
business_context: A logistics route-planning console (React + Mapbox) clusters 12,000 delivery stops client-side. The clustering runs inside render on every pan/zoom event (fired continuously), recomputing a 12k-point k-means each frame and dropping the map to 6fps. Dispatchers planning routes can't pan smoothly; the recompute also spikes GC.
constraints: 12,000 stops; cluster recompute is O(n*k) and ~120ms
Clusters must update when zoom level changes (different granularity)
Panning at the SAME zoom must NOT recompute clusters
Mapbox viewport events fire continuously during gestures
Memory must stay stable (no per-frame array churn)

before_state.description: Clustering runs in the render body keyed on the live viewport, so every pan recomputes the full cluster set.

issues: clusterStops runs on every render including every pan
Depends on lat/lng so panning at the same zoom recomputes
Allocates fresh cluster arrays each frame (GC pressure)

reasoning: Naive fix: useMemo keyed on viewport. Rejected — viewport includes lat/lng, which change on every pan, so the memo cache never hits during panning. Alternative: throttle setViewport. Rejected — clusters then lag the map and panning feels detached; throttling still recomputes on the trailing edge. Chosen: clustering granularity depends only on integer zoom level (not pan position) for a given dataset, so memoize on [stops, Math.floor(zoom)]; pan is handled purely by Mapbox's own viewport without recompute, and a web worker computes clusters off-thread when zoom changes. Cross-layer trace: pan updates Mapbox viewport without touching cluster state (UI) -> cluster memo keyed on quantized zoom only recomputes on zoom change (compute) -> heavy k-means runs in a worker, posting results (async) -> map renders existing clusters during pan (perf) -> stable references avoid GC churn (memory) -> tests assert pan triggers 0 recomputes.

after_state.description: Clusters are memoized on [stops, integer zoom] and computed in a worker on zoom change; panning never recomputes.

files.purpose: Recomputes clusters only when the integer zoom (or dataset) changes, offloading the work to a worker; panning is a no-op.
Runs the O(n*k) clustering off the main thread so even the zoom-change recompute doesn't jank the map.
Tracks only zoom for clustering; panning is handled by Mapbox internally without triggering recompute.

migration_strategy: Introduce useClusters + worker behind a `geo_cluster_worker` flag
Verify cluster output matches the synchronous version at each zoom level
Canary with one dispatch center; measure pan fps and recompute frequency
Ramp to all centers once panning holds 55+ fps
Remove in-render clustering and the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture: 12,000 stops; simulate 30 pan events at constant zoom then one zoom change. Assert output: clusters identical to the reference clusterStops at that zoom. Side-effect assert: 0 worker postMessage calls during the 30 pans; exactly 1 on the zoom change. Memory assert: no growth in retained cluster arrays across pans. Chaos test: rapid zoom in/out 10x and assert only distinct integer zoom levels trigger recompute (cache dedupes repeats).
what_breaks_naive_approach: useMemo keyed on the full viewport never hits cache during panning because lat/lng change every frame; throttling makes clusters lag and still recomputes. Keying on integer zoom (the only thing that changes cluster granularity) plus a worker is what removes per-pan recompute.

title: Fix a Next.js middleware + RSC fetch that disables full-route caching on a high-traffic marketing page
business_context: A marketing landing page (Next.js App Router) was meant to be statically cached but accidentally reads cookies() and uses fetch with cache:'no-store' for an A/B flag, forcing dynamic rendering on every request. The page that should serve from the edge in 30ms now runs the full RSC render (~280ms) per request, and origin CPU is saturated during a campaign spike (50k req/min).
constraints: Page must remain mostly static and edge-cacheable
A/B variant must still be selectable but cached per variant
Cookies are only needed for the variant bucket, nothing else
Campaign traffic up to 50k req/min
Must not leak one user's variant to another

before_state.description: Reading cookies() and a no-store fetch in the page opts the whole route into dynamic rendering on every request.

issues: cookies() forces dynamic rendering for the entire route
cache:'no-store' disables the Data Cache
Every request runs the full RSC render at origin

reasoning: Naive fix: add export const revalidate = 60. Rejected — cookies() still forces dynamic rendering regardless of revalidate, so the route never becomes static. Alternative: read the variant client-side and swap copy after load. Rejected — causes a flash of variant A and hurts LCP/SEO of the headline. Chosen: move variant selection to middleware that rewrites to a per-variant static path (e.g., /landing/a), so each variant is its own cacheable route; the page becomes fully static per variant with revalidated CMS fetch (no cookies in the page). Cross-layer trace: middleware reads the ab cookie and rewrites to /landing/[variant] (edge) -> the [variant] page is statically generated per variant with revalidate (cache) -> CMS fetch uses tag-based revalidation, not no-store (data) -> no cookies() in the page so it stays static (render) -> per-variant cache keys prevent leakage (isolation) -> tests assert the route is statically cached.

after_state.description: Middleware buckets users to a static per-variant route; the page is statically generated with revalidation and no cookie reads.

files.purpose: Selects the A/B variant at the edge and rewrites to a static per-variant path, keeping cookie logic out of the cached page.
Statically generates each variant with a 5-minute revalidate and tag-based invalidation; no cookies, so it stays cacheable.

migration_strategy: Add the middleware rewrite + static [variant] route behind a `landing_static_ab` flag
Deploy to preview; verify each variant route reports static/ISR in build output
Canary 10% of campaign traffic; measure edge cache HIT ratio and origin CPU
Ramp to 100% once cache HIT > 95% and origin CPU drops
Remove the cookie-reading page and the flag; keep tag-based revalidation for CMS edits
testing_strategy: Fixture: request /landing with ab=b cookie. Assert output: middleware rewrites to /landing/b and the served HTML has variant B copy. Side-effect assert: the [variant] route is statically rendered (no per-request RSC at origin) — verify via x-vercel-cache HIT on repeat. Isolation assert: a request with ab=a never receives variant B HTML. Chaos test: invalidate the copy-b tag and assert only variant B revalidates while A stays cached.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Adding revalidate doesn't help because cookies() unconditionally forces dynamic rendering; client-side variant swap flashes variant A and hurts LCP. Moving bucketing to middleware + per-variant static routes is what restores edge caching.

title: Replace a 1.5MB moment.js + lodash footprint with native APIs on a fintech reporting bundle
business_context: A fintech reporting SPA ships moment.js (with all locales, 530KB) and full lodash (70KB) plus moment-timezone data (900KB) in the main bundle. Reports are mostly tables and dates; the date/utility libs dominate the 2.6MB bundle, and analysts on bank-network throttled connections wait 5s for the first report. Tree-shaking is defeated by the default imports.
constraints: Date formatting/parsing must stay correct across timezones used (US/EU)
Existing report code calls dozens of moment()/lodash helpers
Locale support needed only for en, de, fr
Must not change report output formatting
Bundle budget: main chunk under 800KB gzipped

before_state.description: Default imports of moment, moment-timezone, and lodash pull entire libraries and all locale/tz data into the main bundle.

issues: import moment pulls all locales by default
moment-timezone bundles ~900KB of tz data
import _ from 'lodash' defeats tree-shaking

reasoning: Naive fix: import lodash functions individually (lodash/groupBy). Rejected — helps lodash a little but moment + tz data is the bulk (1.4MB) and stays. Alternative: switch to day.js. Rejected — day.js is small but its timezone plugin still bundles IANA data and the migration surface is large; native Intl already covers the formatting/timezone needs. Chosen: replace moment with the native Intl.DateTimeFormat (locale + timeZone aware, zero bundle), use date-fns or plain Date for arithmetic, and replace lodash helpers with native (Object.groupBy / reduce); add a bundle-budget CI gate. Cross-layer trace: formatDate uses Intl.DateTimeFormat with timeZone (compute, 0KB) -> grouping uses a native reduce (compute) -> imports of moment/lodash removed (bundle) -> CI budget enforces <800KB (config) -> output format verified identical via golden tests (correctness) -> rollout module-by-module.

after_state.description: Native Intl + Date replace moment/timezone; native reduce replaces lodash; a CI budget enforces the reduced bundle.

files.purpose: Uses cached Intl.DateTimeFormat (timezone+locale aware, zero bundle cost) and a native reduce for grouping.
Fails the build if the main entrypoint exceeds 800KB and emits stats for regression tracking.

migration_strategy: Migrate format.ts to Intl/native behind a `native_dates` flag, keeping moment path for parity checks
Run golden-output diff tests comparing moment vs Intl formatting for all locales/zones
Canary the new bundle; verify main chunk drops below 800KB and TTI improves
Remove moment/moment-timezone/lodash from package.json once parity holds
Flip the perf budget to error and delete the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture: a table of (iso, zone, locale) tuples covering US Eastern, Berlin, Paris with DST boundaries. Assert output: formatDate equals the previously-captured moment output string-for-string. Side-effect assert: the built main chunk excludes moment/lodash (stats.json) and is < 800KB. Negative test: an invalid ISO yields 'Invalid Date' handling identical to before. Chaos test: a DST transition timestamp (spring-forward) formats to the same wall-clock string as the legacy code.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Per-function lodash imports leave the 1.4MB moment+tz data in place, and day.js still bundles IANA data with a large migration. Native Intl.DateTimeFormat provides timezone-aware formatting at zero bundle cost, which is the only way to hit the budget.

title: Stop a SvelteKit feed from invalidating all derived stores on each new post via fine-grained reactivity
business_context: A social feed (SvelteKit) keeps the post list in a writable store; a derived store computes per-post 'time ago' and an aggregate 'unread count'. Each new post (arriving ~2/sec via SSE) updates the list store, recomputing the derived 'time ago' for all 500 cached posts and re-rendering the whole feed. Scroll stutters at 10fps during active threads.
constraints: Up to 500 posts cached, 2 new posts/sec via SSE
'time ago' must update at least every 60s
Unread count must be accurate in real time
SSR for the initial feed page
Cannot block scroll during inserts

before_state.description: A derived store recomputes formatted time for every post whenever the list changes, and the feed re-renders all cards.

issues: decorated recomputes ago for all 500 posts on any list change
Each map creates new object identities, re-rendering every card
timeAgo recomputed even for posts whose minute didn't change

files.content:
<script>
  import { decorated } from '$lib/stores';
</script>
{#each $decorated as p (p.id)}
  <article>{p.text} <small>{p.ago}</small></article>
{/each}
issues: Re-renders all cards when decorated changes identity
Keyed each helps reorder but new object refs still update every card

reasoning: Naive fix: throttle the SSE inserts. Rejected — reduces frequency but each insert still recomputes 'ago' for 500 posts and re-renders all cards. Alternative: compute 'ago' inside each card component reactively. Rejected — better, but if it reads a shared 'now' store updated each second it re-renders all cards every second. Chosen: prepend new posts immutably without remapping existing ones (keep their object identity), compute 'ago' per-card from createdAt against a coarse 'now' that ticks every 30s (so cards update at most twice a minute), and derive unread incrementally; Svelte's keyed each then only mounts the new card. Cross-layer trace: SSE prepends to posts preserving existing refs (data) -> keyed each mounts only the new card (UI) -> each card computes ago from a 30s-tick now store (reactivity) -> unread derived from a counter updated on insert/read (state) -> SSR initial render unchanged (render) -> tests assert one card mounts per insert.

after_state.description: Inserts preserve existing post identities; cards compute 'ago' from a coarse 30s clock; unread is tracked incrementally; keyed each mounts only new cards.

files.purpose: Prepends posts without remapping existing ones, exposes a 30s clock, and tracks unread incrementally instead of re-scanning.

files.content:
<script>
  import { now } from '$lib/stores';
  export let post;
  $: ago = relTime(post.createdAt, $now);
</script>
<article>{post.text} <small>{ago}</small></article>
files.purpose: Computes 'ago' per card from the coarse clock, so a new insert never recomputes time for existing cards.

files.content:
<script>
  import { posts } from '$lib/stores';
  import PostCard from './PostCard.svelte';
</script>
{#each $posts as p (p.id)}
  <PostCard post={p} />
{/each}
files.purpose: Renders the raw posts with stable keys so Svelte mounts only the newly prepended card.

migration_strategy: Introduce prependPost + coarse clock + per-card ago behind a `feed_finegrained` flag
Verify 'ago' values match the legacy derived output within the 30s tolerance
Canary on active threads; measure scroll fps during inserts
Ramp to 100% once inserts cause one card mount and scroll holds 55fps
Remove the global decorated store and the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture: 500 posts; prepend 10 posts at 2/sec. Assert output: each insert mounts exactly one new PostCard (component lifecycle spy); existing cards are not recreated. Side-effect assert: 'ago' updates at most every 30s, not on each insert. Correctness assert: unread increments per unread insert and decrements on markRead. Chaos test: prepend a burst of 50 posts in 1s and assert the list is capped at 500 and only the visible new cards mount (no full re-render).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Throttling inserts still remaps and re-renders all 500 cards per insert; a shared per-second 'now' would re-render every card each second. Preserving identities + a coarse 30s clock per card is what keeps inserts O(1) in renders.

title: Fix an over-eager IntersectionObserver lazy-loader that mounts 400 below-fold images at once
business_context: An e-commerce category page lazy-loads product images with an IntersectionObserver, but the root margin is set to '2000px' and threshold 0, so on initial load nearly all 400 below-fold images enter the observer and begin loading simultaneously, saturating the connection and delaying the above-fold LCP image by 2.2s. Mobile users on 3G see blank tiles for seconds.
constraints: 400 product images per category page
Above-the-fold images must load first (LCP)
Below-fold images load just-in-time as the user nears them
Limit concurrent image requests to avoid connection saturation
Must work without next/image (custom CDN component)

before_state.description: A huge rootMargin triggers nearly all images to load at once on first paint, starving the LCP image of bandwidth.

issues: rootMargin 2000px makes ~all images intersect at load
400 images start loading simultaneously, saturating bandwidth
LCP image competes with 399 below-fold requests

reasoning: Naive fix: lower rootMargin to 200px. Rejected — better, but a fast scroll still triggers dozens of concurrent loads with no cap, and there's no prioritization of the LCP image. Alternative: rely solely on native loading='lazy'. Rejected — browser heuristics vary and still don't cap concurrency or prioritize LCP, and we need deterministic behavior for the CDN component. Chosen: a shared lazy-load queue with a concurrency limit (e.g., 6), a modest rootMargin (300px), and explicit priority for above-the-fold images (eager + fetchpriority high), so below-fold images load just-in-time and throttled. Cross-layer trace: above-fold images marked eager + fetchpriority=high (LCP) -> below-fold images observed with 300px margin (timing) -> intersections enqueue into a global limiter of 6 concurrent loads (network) -> images load as slots free (perf) -> CDN component unchanged API (compat) -> tests assert concurrency cap respected.

after_state.description: Above-fold images are eager/high-priority; below-fold images intersect with a small margin and load through a concurrency-limited queue.

files.purpose: Global limiter that caps simultaneous image loads at 6, preventing connection saturation.
Eagerly high-priority-loads above-fold images and throttles below-fold loads through the shared queue with a 300px margin.

migration_strategy: Ship the queue + priority flag behind a `lazy_img_queue` toggle
Mark the first row of category images priority; below-fold use the queue
Canary on mobile; measure LCP and image request waterfall
Ramp to 100% once LCP < 2.5s on 3G and no request storm
Remove the 2000px-margin loader and the toggle
testing_strategy: Fixture: render a category grid of 400 LazyImg, first 6 priority. Assert output: the 6 priority images get src immediately with fetchpriority=high; the rest have no src at load. Side-effect assert: scrolling to reveal images never exceeds 6 concurrent in-flight loads (queue active counter). LCP assert (Lighthouse): the hero image is the LCP and loads first. Chaos test: instantly scroll to the bottom and assert images still load capped at 6 concurrent (no 400-request burst).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Lowering rootMargin reduces but doesn't cap concurrency, and a fast scroll still floods the connection; native lazy alone doesn't prioritize the LCP image. A concurrency queue plus explicit above-fold priority is required.

title: Eliminate prop-identity churn from a context value that re-renders a 60-field insurance form on every keystroke
business_context: An insurance quote form (React) has 60 fields wired to a FormContext whose value (values + setField + errors) is recreated on every render. Typing in one field re-renders all 60 inputs plus their validation message components, dropping typing to ~12fps and causing cursor jumps. Agents entering claims abandon the form mid-entry; completion rate fell 7%.
constraints: 60 fields across 5 sections; per-field validation
Typing in one field must not re-render the other 59
Cross-field validation (e.g., end date > start date) must still run
Submit must collect all field values atomically
React 18; form library swap not allowed this quarter

before_state.description: The context provider recreates its value object each render and stores all values in one object, so any field change re-renders every consumer.

issues: value object recreated each render
setField recreated each render (unstable identity)
Every field reads the whole values object, so one change re-renders all
Subscribes to the entire values/errors objects
Re-renders on any field's change

reasoning: Naive fix: useMemo the context value. Rejected — values changes on every keystroke so the memo's dep changes and all consumers still re-render. Alternative: split a context per field. Rejected — 60 providers is unwieldy and cross-field validation gets awkward. Chosen: store form state in a ref-backed external store (useSyncExternalStore) and have each Field subscribe to only its own value+error via a selector; setField is stable; cross-field validation runs in the store and notifies only affected fields. Cross-layer trace: store holds values/errors in a ref (state) -> setField mutates and notifies just the changed field's listeners (data) -> Field subscribes to its own slice via getSnapshot (UI) -> cross-field rule notifies the dependent field too (validation) -> submit reads the whole snapshot atomically (data) -> stable setField identity (perf) -> tests assert one field renders per keystroke.

after_state.description: Form state lives in an external store; each Field subscribes only to its value+error; setField is stable; cross-field validation notifies just the affected fields.

files.purpose: Per-field subscriptions and stable setField; cross-field validation notifies only the dependent field.
Provides a single stable store instance, so the context value never changes identity.
Subscribes to only its own field state so typing in one field never re-renders the others.

migration_strategy: Introduce the external form store behind a `form_field_store` flag, keeping context-object path
Verify cross-field validation parity (date rule) against the old form
Canary with a subset of agents; measure typing fps and cursor-jump reports
Ramp to 100% once typing holds 60fps and no cursor jumps
Remove the object-context provider and the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture: render a 60-field form with per-field render counters; type into field #5. Assert output: only field #5 (and any cross-field dependent) re-renders; the other 58 do not. Side-effect assert: setField identity is stable across renders. Validation assert: setting endDate before startDate sets the endDate error and re-renders only that field. Submit assert: snapshot() returns all 60 values atomically. Chaos test: type rapidly across 10 fields and assert total renders == number of keystrokes (+ validation), no fan-out.
what_breaks_naive_approach: useMemo on the context value can't help because values changes every keystroke, invalidating the memo; per-field contexts don't scale to 60 fields with cross-field rules. An external store with per-field subscriptions is the scalable fix.

title: Reduce TBT on a Next.js dashboard by moving heavy analytics scripts to a partytown-style worker
business_context: A B2B SaaS dashboard loads 4 third-party scripts (analytics, heatmap, chat widget, session-replay) in the document head; together they execute ~900ms of JS on the main thread during load, pushing Total Blocking Time to 720ms and delaying interactivity of the dashboard's primary controls. Customers on lower-end laptops complain the app 'hangs' right after load.
constraints: The 4 third-party scripts must keep functioning (events tracked)
Primary dashboard controls must be interactive within 1.5s
Cannot remove the vendors (contractual analytics requirements)
Next.js App Router with the Script component available
Session-replay must still capture DOM mutations

before_state.description: Third-party scripts load with default strategy in the head, executing on the main thread and blocking interactivity.

issues: All four scripts execute on the main thread during load
Default strategy blocks hydration/interactivity
~900ms of vendor JS contributing to TBT

reasoning: Naive fix: add strategy='lazyOnload' to all four. Rejected — it defers them but they still run on the main thread when they fire, and session-replay/analytics that must capture early events would miss them. Alternative: self-host and bundle the vendors. Rejected — violates vendor terms and still runs on the main thread. Chosen: offload the analytics/heatmap/chat to a web worker via Partytown (strategy='worker'), keeping them off the main thread, while session-replay (needs synchronous DOM access) stays on the main thread but loaded with strategy='afterInteractive' so it runs after the dashboard is interactive. Cross-layer trace: layout configures Partytown (config) -> analytics/heatmap/chat run in a worker (offload) -> postMessage proxies their DOM/network access (bridge) -> session-replay loads afterInteractive on main thread (correctness) -> primary controls hydrate first (perf) -> tests assert main-thread vendor time drops.

after_state.description: Three vendor scripts run in a Partytown worker; session-replay loads afterInteractive on the main thread; primary controls hydrate first.

files.purpose: Runs analytics/heatmap/chat in a worker (off main thread) and loads session-replay after interactivity so it still captures the DOM.
Enables Next's Partytown integration so worker-strategy scripts execute off the main thread.

migration_strategy: Enable nextScriptWorkers and move 3 vendors to worker strategy behind a `scripts_worker` flag
Verify analytics/heatmap/chat still fire events from the worker (vendor dashboards show data)
Canary 10%; measure TBT and the main-thread time attributed to vendor JS
Ramp to 100% once TBT < 200ms and vendor event parity confirmed
Remove the head-blocking scripts and the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture: load the dashboard with the four scripts mocked. Assert output: analytics/heatmap/chat scripts run in the Partytown worker (no main-thread execution entry in the performance timeline). Side-effect assert: dataLayer.push events still reach the vendor (forwarded). Interactivity assert (Lighthouse): TBT < 200ms and primary control click-handler attaches before vendor scripts settle. Chaos test: a vendor script throws on init; assert it fails inside the worker without blocking the main thread or breaking the dashboard.
what_breaks_naive_approach: lazyOnload still runs vendor JS on the main thread when it fires and risks missing early analytics events; self-hosting violates terms and still blocks the thread. Worker offload via Partytown is what removes the main-thread vendor cost.

title: Replace per-cell formatter recreation in an AG-Grid-like data grid causing 200ms scroll jank
business_context: A custom virtualized data grid (React) renders 30 columns x ~40 visible rows. Each cell creates its column's formatter (currency, date, percent) inside the cell render, so during scroll (which re-renders visible cells) thousands of Intl/format objects are allocated per frame, causing 200ms GC pauses and visible jank. Analysts scrolling wide financial grids report stutter.
constraints: 30 columns, ~40 visible rows, fast scroll re-renders cells
Formatters: currency, date, percent, with locale support
Cell values must keep current formatting output
Grid is virtualized (only visible cells render)
No third-party grid; custom implementation

before_state.description: Each cell instantiates its formatter on render, allocating thousands of formatter objects per scroll frame.

issues: Creates a new Intl formatter on every cell render
Thousands of allocations per scroll frame
GC pauses cause scroll jank

reasoning: Naive fix: useMemo the formatter in each cell. Rejected — useMemo runs per cell instance and still re-allocates on first render of each newly-virtualized cell during scroll; cells churn constantly while scrolling. Alternative: precompute all formatted strings up front. Rejected — for large datasets that wastes memory formatting offscreen cells. Chosen: a module-level formatter cache keyed on [type, locale, currency] shared across all cells, so each cell looks up a cached formatter (created once) instead of constructing one; cells become pure and memoized. Cross-layer trace: a getFormatter cache returns a shared Intl instance (compute) -> Cell looks it up and formats (UI) -> no per-render allocation during scroll (memory) -> memoized Cell skips re-render when value/type unchanged (perf) -> locale changes create one new cached formatter (correctness) -> tests assert formatter instances are reused.

after_state.description: A shared module-level formatter cache returns one Intl instance per (type, locale); cells are memoized and allocation-free during scroll.

files.purpose: Caches one Intl formatter per (type, locale) so cells never allocate formatters during scroll.
Memoized cell that reuses cached formatters and skips re-render when value/type/locale are unchanged.

migration_strategy: Introduce the formatter cache + memoized Cell behind a `grid_formatter_cache` flag
Verify formatted output matches the per-cell formatter byte-for-byte across types/locales
Canary on wide grids; measure scroll fps and GC pause counts
Ramp to 100% once scroll holds 55+ fps with no >50ms GC pauses
Remove per-cell formatter construction and the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture: render a 30x40 visible grid; scroll to re-render visible cells 50 times. Assert output: cell text matches the reference formatter for currency/percent/date. Side-effect assert: the number of Intl formatter constructions stays constant (== distinct type/locale combos), not per render (constructor spy). GC assert: no per-frame allocation spike during scroll (heap delta near zero). Chaos test: switch locale mid-scroll and assert exactly one new formatter per new (type, locale) is created, not thousands.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Per-cell useMemo still allocates a formatter for each newly-virtualized cell during scroll, so the allocation storm continues; precomputing all strings wastes memory on offscreen rows. A shared module cache is the only allocation-free approach.

title: Stop a React Native (Hermes) FlatList from re-rendering all rows on selection in a retail POS app
business_context: A retail point-of-sale app (React Native, Hermes) shows a product FlatList of ~600 items. Tapping an item to add to cart updates a selectedIds set in parent state passed as a prop, so all visible rows re-render on each tap; on older POS tablets this causes a 250ms tap-to-feedback delay, slowing checkout lines. Cashiers tap rapidly and the lag compounds.
constraints: ~600 products; cashiers tap rapidly during checkout
Tap feedback must appear within 100ms
Runs on low-end Android POS tablets (Hermes engine)
Cart total must update on each tap
FlatList virtualization must remain (don't render all 600)

before_state.description: selectedIds is passed to each row and the renderItem closure changes each render, so all visible rows re-render on every tap.

issues: renderItem inline closure recreated each render
selected.has(item.id) passed to all rows, so all re-render on any tap
onPress closure new each render, defeating Row memo

reasoning: Naive fix: wrap Row in React.memo. Rejected — renderItem and onPress are recreated each render, so memo never short-circuits. Alternative: store selection in each Row's local state. Rejected — the cart total lives in the parent and would desync from per-row local state. Chosen: keep selection in an external store; each Row subscribes to only its own selected boolean; renderItem and onPress are stabilized with useCallback; extraData not needed because rows self-subscribe. Cross-layer trace: store holds selectedIds + total (state) -> Row subscribes to its own id's boolean (UI) -> stable toggle from the store (perf) -> stable renderItem via useCallback (FlatList) -> cart total derived in the store (data) -> tests assert one row re-renders per tap.

after_state.description: Selection lives in an external store; each Row subscribes to its own boolean; toggle and renderItem are stable; only the tapped row re-renders.

files.purpose: Per-id selection subscriptions plus a derived total so a tap notifies only the affected row and the total.
Subscribes to only its own selected boolean so a tap re-renders just this row, with a stable onPress.
Stabilizes renderItem so FlatList doesn't re-render rows on parent updates; rows self-subscribe for selection.

migration_strategy: Introduce the selection store + self-subscribing rows behind a `pos_selection_store` flag
Test on a low-end POS tablet; verify tap-to-feedback latency
Canary in a few stores; measure tap latency and checkout throughput
Ramp to all stores once tap feedback < 100ms
Remove the prop-passed selection and the flag
testing_strategy: Fixture: render 600 products in a FlatList with per-row render counters; tap item #120. Assert output: only row #120 re-renders; the total updates. Side-effect assert: toggle adjusts the store total by the product price; onPress identity is stable across renders. Performance assert: tap handler-to-state update < 16ms on a Hermes profiler. Chaos test: tap 30 items rapidly and assert total renders == 30 (one per tap), total reflects all 30 prices.
what_breaks_naive_approach: React.memo on Row is defeated because renderItem and onPress are recreated each render; per-row local state desyncs from the parent cart total. An external store with per-id subscriptions and stable callbacks isolates each tap.
